#### ↳ Re: ProCobol Outer join

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-08T22:25:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mmg7245hbpg6m4ia9t8k3qd48l9mv3598t@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c06426f2-e7e7-428c-9350-b3343d6a612c@k13g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 8 May 2008 06:58:32 -0700 (PDT), jeff <jmoore207@gmail.com> wrote:

>I am having a problem with a left outer join when I add conditionals.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>regardless. Any ideas?

You might find ANS syntax more intuitive. It is available on Oracle 9 and above.

Select
        A. vehicle,
        B.Vehicle,
        B.acct,
        B.MOYR,
        B.Trancode
 from  Table1 A LEFT OUTER JOIN Table2 B
 where A.vehicle=B.vehicle
  and   B.acct='1234'
  and   B.MOYR = '0502'
  and  B.trancode='80'
  order by a.vehicle

What if a vehicle appears in B but not in A? It cannot be done easily with the old syntax;
it's a snap with ANS syntax. Just change LEFT OUTER JOIN to FULL OUTER JOIN.
```

<a id="t-24aa01c718"></a>
## Thread: Active development/RAD/Extreme programming (not to be taken seriously) — 4 msgs

### Active development/RAD/Extreme programming (not to be taken seriously)

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-09T17:29:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xh4Vj.16$xF6.15@newsfe20.lga>`

```
I always think that there's nothing new in programming & computers
generally: "plus ca change" etc..  The methods espoused by PD et al sound
very much like the way we used to program, right back at the beginning of
time: bash something together, see how it works, make corrections, add
functionality - see?  Don't bother about planning, develop incrementally,
refine continuously, etc.  The only significant difference is that modern
methods involve the users far more frequently - as long as they have the
time.  Use of code-generators and all the current tools simply make the
process faster: modern computer power makes it possible to discover and fix
problems much more easily and quickly.  But what else is different?????


PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Active development/RAD/Extreme programming (not to be taken seriously)

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-09T19:48:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgq9249ubklslo51kislqu3phnb9pjk7sl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Xh4Vj.16$xF6.15@newsfe20.lga>`

```
On Fri, 9 May 2008 17:29:09 -0500, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>I always think that there's nothing new in programming & computers
>generally: "plus ca change" etc..  The methods espoused by PD et al sound
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>problems much more easily and quickly.  But what else is different?????

Now

1. Active has a name and written methodology
2. Airline magazines (and lesser authorities) say it is HOT
3. Computers are cheap
4. Users are involved
5. Today's highly competitive market, where only geniuses survive

In the old days

1. A bunch of inarticulate geeks scrambling to get something working
2. No alternatives
3. Computers were an expensive 'investment', not an expense
4. Developers thought they knew the business
5. Monopolies, which didn't need to be competitive

Do you see the difference? It's all in spin and self-delusion.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Active development/RAD/Extreme programming (not to be taken seriously)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-11T04:23:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<68m0f9F2tovp2U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<Xh4Vj.16$xF6.15@newsfe20.lga> <cgq9249ubklslo51kislqu3phnb9pjk7sl@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:cgq9249ubklslo51kislqu3phnb9pjk7sl@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 9 May 2008 17:29:09 -0500, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Except that tangible results can be quantified and are NOT just spin and 
delusion.

When one system delivers stuff that is timely and useful, and the other 
doesn't, then I think it's a no brainer.

Having participated in a number of successful implementations and celebrated 
with users the delivery of results they can actually use, I reckon it is 
real.

But, of course, I may be deluded...

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Active development/RAD/Extreme programming (not to be taken seriously)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-10T12:52:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<68k9ubF2t8jo0U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<Xh4Vj.16$xF6.15@newsfe20.lga>`

```


"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:Xh4Vj.16$xF6.15@newsfe20.lga...
>I always think that there's nothing new in programming & computers
> generally: "plus ca change" etc..  The methods espoused by PD et al sound
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You said not to take this seriously, Peter.

I think it is a perfectly valid serious statement. I fnd myself in agreement 
with it.

Of course, we know a lot more now than we did then, and some of the things 
that were missing then, are now available.

For myself, I'm interested in what works, whether it fits the perceived 
wisdom or not.

Having tried these approaches over a number of years now, I'm satisfied that 
it works, so for me, it makes sense to do it that way.

Pete.
```

## Thread: ProCobol Outer join — 2 msgs

### Re: ProCobol Outer join

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-10T10:57:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<48257FB0.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
>>> On 5/8/2008 at 9:25 PM, in message
<mmg7245hbpg6m4ia9t8k3qd48l9mv3598t@4ax.com>, Robert<no@e.mail> wrote:
> On Thu, 8 May 2008 06:58:32 -0700 (PDT), jeff <jmoore207@gmail.com>
wrote:
> 
>>I am having a problem with a left outer join when I add conditionals.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> JOIN. 

I don't know about Oracle, but in DB2 the syntax would be

Select
        A.vehicle,
        B.Vehicle,
        B.acct,
        B.MOYR,
        B.Trancode
 from  Table1 A LEFT OUTER JOIN Table2 B 
                on A.vehicle=B.vehicle
 where B.acct='1234'
  and  B.MOYR = '0502'
  and  B.trancode='80'
 order by a.vehicle

I think.  Not an expert, and i didn't test it.

Frank
```

## Thread: Altova v2008r2 — 1 msgs

### Altova v2008r2

- **From:** announce@altova.com
- **Date:** 2008-05-12T11:03:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eb0ed651-4b4a-45b3-98d5-1d6275751ec7@x41g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Greetings!

Just a brief note to let you know about Release 2 of Altova's v2008
product line. From very large file support, to new functionality for
working with OOXML and more, a few of the new features are:

* Very large file support in XMLSpy XML editor
* Support for Java, C#, JavaScript & VBScript in the XSLT engines in
XMLSpy
* Advanced find & replace in XML Schema editor in XMLSpy
* Support for (integration with and code gen for) Visual Studio 2008
in XMLSpy
* Mapping Excel 2007 (OOXML) data to XML, DBs, EDI, etc., in MapForce
* Word 2007 (OOXML)  output from XML and DBs in StyleVision
* BPMN diagrams in UModel
* Diff/merge support for Office 2007 (OOXML) docs in DiffDog
* Global Resources support across multiple tools
* And much more

More details, including screenshots: http://www.altova.com/whatsnew.html
Download an upgrade or free trial: http://www.altova.com/download.html
```

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### ASP.NET compilation in netExpress 5.0

- **From:** dmbaum@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2008-05-12T13:14:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<34ec9521-9ce3-4c98-993d-83f856377510@y38g2000hsy.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello,

I am unable to compile any Cobol ASP.NET applications in the Net
Express University Edition.

It is apparently installed OK,  and I have managed to get Cobol .Net
windows applications to compile and run.

However, the system will not compile .net web form apps in Cobol.

VB .Net and C# web forms work OK.

When trying to compile even the skeleton application I get to errors
below.

Can anyone help? I have tried the Microfocus support board and have
received no answer after several months.

This is my first posting to this group. I am a professional programmer
(among other things) and I am learning Cobol for fun. I have been
studying older versions of the language as well as the latest object
oriented Cobol .Net. Frankly I think the object oriented version of
the language is both fascinating a bit bonkers. The idea of writing
web applications in Cobol is very appealing (just so I can say I've
done it) so I would very much like to fix this error.


Thanks.

Daniel
```

<a id="t-04abe511d9"></a>
## Thread: CICS web server — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### CICS web server

- **From:** chuck_leviton@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2008-05-12T13:33:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2d06cc4-e24f-4afc-9c92-5234e435dfb9@59g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
I posted this on the bit.listserv.ibm-main forum but haven't seen any
response, so am trying here

My question has to do with the built in HTTP Server in CICS (not the
CICS Webservices that became available with 3.1 and later).  We have
just started to use this to web enable some existing transactions.
Gosh the default web page looks ugly!.  Our intent is not to display
it on the browser but to trap the HTTP stream and process it in some
fashion, so it doesn't matter.

One of the transactions we have is not really BMS based, it doesn't do
a SEND MAP but just a SEND and sends more than a screen full of data.

EXEC CICS
    SEND FROM(OUT-AREA)
         LENGTH(3760)
END-EXEC.

Currently there is a C program that interacts with it (not sure
how, does some kind of terminal emulation or something like that)
which gets all the data it sends.

But when we invoke this tran via the web browser we are getting only
the "first screen" of data, ie just the first 1920 bytes.

What need we do to get the full 3760 bytes?
```

## Thread: Validating input file — 32 msgs

### Validating input file

- **From:** jeff <jmoore207@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-13T06:37:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as

 FD  DISCFILE.
 01  DISC-REC               PIC X(129).
 01  DISC-REC2 REDEFINES DISC-REC.
     03  DISC-RECR OCCURS 129  PIC X.

It is a comma-delimited file with 16 fields (15 commas). I need to
check and make sure that each line is only 129 characters and I assume
1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?
```

```

> I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?


How can I check 130 for LF?

Can I use an evaluate? or will it be a problem because the record is
defined as 129?
```

#### ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-13T10:10:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kPGdnfI6I7HDPLTVnZ2dnUVZ_vudnZ2d@posted.mid-floridainternet>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```

"jeff" <jmoore207@gmail.com> wrote in message
news:30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
> I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?

If this is truly a fixed-length record on disk, then the
LF should appear in the last position; if the file is "line
sequential" then the SELECT clause for the file
should reflect that and the record will be filled with
trailing spaces when it is read into the program. In other
words, the record, in memory, will always have
128 characters and a LF or 129 characters, depending.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-13T11:22:00-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Zmdncv2O9qFL7TVnZ2dnUVZWhednZ2d@posted.mid-floridainternet>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <kPGdnfI6I7HDPLTVnZ2dnUVZ_vudnZ2d@posted.mid-floridainternet> <29ae81a8-ee4c-4fdf-84c4-84baeb7f1292@e39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```

"jeff" <jmoore207@gmail.com> wrote in message
news:29ae81a8-ee4c-4fdf-84c4-84baeb7f1292@e39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com...
>On May 13, 10:10 am, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
>> "jeff" <jmoore...@gmail.com> wrote in message
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>It is a comma delimited file and can have blank fields

With Micro Focus, I might choose the following:

        input-output section.
        file-control.
            select discfile assign to ...
                organization line sequential.

The use of "line sequential" allows the reading of variable
length lines of text. When each "line" is read, the record,
in memory, will have each of the characters from the text
"line" followed by some number of spaces to fill out the
record; but will not have the LF (or new line) separator.

What compiler are you using? That will determine how
to set up the program to read the file.
```

- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <k89j24tkdc8hvkt9focf510rtvp889f3ps@4ax.com> <f0907ddf-24fd-48d4-b115-a223125e8ec6@l64g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 13 May 2008 07:29:57 -0700 (PDT), jeff <jmoore207@gmail.com>
wrote:

>> Are you reading an ASCII file from one Windows machine to another
>> Windows machine?
>
>Unix ascii file

I was surprised when I used TextWrangler on the Mac to discover all
the different formats it recognized - including Mac, Windows, Unix
(Mac uses Unix) with different line-end/form-feed combinations.
Someone here will tell what Unix files expect.

I've worked on various mainframes and almost all of them handle record
length with the OS.   A fixed 80 column record is 80 columns.  Period.
It is interesting that this is from a time when space cost more, but
it gave up space in the name of speed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** Alain Reymond <arwebmail@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2008-05-13T17:15:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4829b094$0$2998$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<f0907ddf-24fd-48d4-b115-a223125e8ec6@l64g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <k89j24tkdc8hvkt9focf510rtvp889f3ps@4ax.com> <f0907ddf-24fd-48d4-b115-a223125e8ec6@l64g2000hse.googlegroups.com>`

```


jeff a ï¿½crit :
> On May 13, 10:27 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
>   
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>   
Jeff, if you use line sequential files, you should not have to care 
about the LF. The records will be 129 chars long.
Then, use unstring :
1 my-record.
    2 my-field pic(129) occurs 16.
....

                   unstring DISC-REC
                       delimited by ";" or "," or x'09'
                       into
                            my-field(01),
                            my-field(02),
                            my-field(03),
                            my-field(04),
                            my-field(05),
                            my-field(06),
                            my-field(07),
                            my-field(08),
                            my-field(09),
                            my-field(10),
                            my-field(11),
                            my-field(12),
                            my-field(13),
                            my-field(14),
                            my-field(15),
                            my-field(16)
                   end-unstring


Regards.

Alain
```

```
Alain Reymond wrote:
> jeff a �crit :
>> On May 13, 10:27 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Or, my favored technique:

    MOVE 1 TO DISC-POINTER.
    PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > 16
        UNSTRING DISC-REC DELIMITED BY ',' INTO MY-FIELD(I)
            WITH POINTER DISC-POINTER
    END-PERFORM
```

```
On May 13, 11:44 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> Do you need to verify that you only have the 16 commas?  (None of the
> data contain commas)?

I am already checking that there is 15 characters for the 16 fields.

       Validate-File-
Format.
           Read
DISCFILE
               AT END MOVE "Y" TO WS-
EOF
           End-
Read
           Perform until WS-EOF =
"Y"
                   inspect DISC-REC tallying ws-comma-
count
                   for all
","
               If WS-Comma-Count <> 15                (file has to
have 16 colums)
                   perform display-100 thru display-100-
exit
                   go to 0400-
eoj
               End-
If
               Perform Parse-The-Record  (Here I wanna check and make
sure it is not > 129)
               Read Discfile at
end
                   Move "Y" to WS-
EOF
               End-
Read
           End-
Perform.
           CLOSE
DISCFILE.
       Validate-File-Format-Exit.
Exit.
```

```
"jeff" <jmoore207@gmail.com> wrote in message
news:7c26098b-3c55-438e-8aa6-f33f53cf16d2@59g2000hsb.googlegroups.com...
>On May 13, 12:46 pm, "Rick Smith" <ricksm...@mfi.net> wrote:
>> "jeff" <jmoore...@gmail.com> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>130 as a line feed if 129 is not spaces

It depends on the file type defined by the ORGANIZATION
clause. Line sequential organization, in Micro Focus, Fujitsu,
et al., will not place the line feed into the record.

What the compiler, you are using, will do depends on that
compiler.
```

```
000048     SELECT
DISCFILE                                              06283165
000050         ASSIGN TO DYNAMIC
WKFILE                                 06283165
000051         ORGANIZATION IS
SEQUENTIAL                               06283165
000052         Access Mode Is
Sequential                                06283165
000053         File Status Is File-
Status.                              06283165
```

```
>000051         ORGANIZATION IS
>SEQUENTIAL                               06283165

Sequential may contain binary data and will be either
fixed length or, if variable length, will have a header
record and a length prefix for each record. This is not
typical for files containing records with CSVs.

Use ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
```

```
With Micro Focus
   ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL

can be either line sequential or record sequential - depending the compiler 
directive, SEQUENTIAL
```

#### ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-13T10:07:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JPWdnUvysMkxM7TVnZ2dnUVZ_tPinZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
jeff wrote:
> I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?

First, look at the file with a hex editor to validate your assumptions.

The usual purpose of a comma-delimited file is to dispense with imbedded 
blanks (else the file would have been defined as fixed length in the first 
place).

I suspect the file, if really comma-delimited, will NOT be a uniform 129 
bytes.

Going back to your question, what will you do if the record is NOT 129 (or 
130) bytes?

Assuming a variable length record, made up of variable length fields, your 
code should look like:

READ DISCFILE
UNSTRING DISC-REC DELIMITED BY ',' INTO
  FIELD-1
  FIELD-2
  FIELD-3
  ...
  FIELD-15
```

#### ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** Graham Hobbs <ghobbs@cdpwise.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-13T21:37:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ibgk24pu7alnnvukmncpcrsh29fujgnh61@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
If you've got access to REXX I'd try the following rather than messing
with Cobol and 'unknown' file inputs ..

fi = 'path/filename'
fo = 'newfilename'
do while lines (fi)
   wsin = call filein(fi)
   le = length (wsin)
   if le = 129 then do	
      call lineout fo, wsin
   end
   else do
      if le > 129 then do
         say 'am over 129, what do i do'
      end
      else do
         newle = 129 - le
         newrec = wsin||newle(spaces)  .. forget the syntax
         call lineout fo, newrec
      end
   end
end
call lineout (fi)
call lineout (fo)

.. now they're all 129 for sure.
.. if my code's OK!

Graham Hobbs
```

```
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>.. if my code's OK!

Procrustes (the stretcher) ..  is a figure from Greek mythology. He was a bandit from
Attica, with a stronghold in the hills outside Eleusis. There, he had an iron bed into
which he invited every passerby to lie down. If the guest proved too tall, he would
amputate the excess length; victims who were too short were stretched on the rack until
they were long enough. Nobody ever fit in the bed because it was secretly adjustable:
Procrustes would stretch or shrink it upon sizing his victims from afar. Procrustes
continued his reign of terror until he was captured by Theseus, who "fitted" Procrustes to
his own bed and cut off his head and feet 

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Procrustes
```

#### ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T02:21:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C0sWj.727942$Gl5.618335@fe02.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
I am still not %100 sure exactly what you want (after reading all the thread). 
However, if you are reading in "line sequential" input and you want to verify 
that each "line" is exactly 129 characters long (before the "end-of-line 
indicators") and you are using any Micro Focus product, I believe the following 
will do:

Select DISCFILE Assign to whatever
     Organization is Line sequential ...
FD  DiscFile
       Record contains 1 to 4096 Characters   *> or any other random large 
maximum size
          Depending on WS-Char-Cnt.
> 01  DISC-REC               PIC X(129).
> 01  DISC-REC2 REDEFINES DISC-REC.
>     03  DISC-RECR OCCURS 4096  PIC X.  *> same large max
         ...
Working-Storage Section.
  ... 01 WS-Char-Cnt   Pic 9(05) Comp-5.

Procedure division.
      ...
Read DiscFile
 If WS-Char-Cnt = 129  Then
     Perform EOL-is-129
Else
    Perform Not-129-Line-Sequential
End-if

  ****

Now, if you want to do other "validation" of the fields that are comma 
delimited, you do need other approaches and if the comma delimited fields MAY 
actually be quoted fields that include embedded commas, then you REALLY will 
need to "loop thru" each byte of the record as this is a fairly complex job.
```

```
verify

> indicators") and you are using any Micro Focus product, I believe the
following
> will do:

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>           Depending on WS-Char-Cnt.

RECORD VARYING DEPENDING WS-Char-Cnt

is sufficient and the record description entry need not have
an OCCURS clause.
```

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>

- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 13 May 2008 06:37:11 -0700 (PDT), jeff <jmoore207@gmail.com> wrote:

>I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?

move 1 to parser
perform  with test after varying word-count from 1 by 1 
  until parser greater than length of disc-rec
    unstring disc-rec delimited by ',' into a-word with pointer parser
   if  word-count not greater than 16
      move a-word to my-word (word-count)
  end-if
end-perform
if  word-count not equal to 16
    display 'wrong number of words ' word-count ' in ' disc-rec
end-if
```

#### ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T00:11:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95sk24dvp3dor42vvmao01l4vhefe7s301@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 13 May 2008 06:37:11 -0700 (PDT), jeff <jmoore207@gmail.com> wrote:

>I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?

awk -F, 'NF!=16' filename

If you want to be more professional

awk -F, 'NF!=16 {print "'Bad record. Bad BAD record. " NF " " $0; exit 1}' filename
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Validating input file

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <mmattias@talsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T08:38:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6YBWj.4368$nW2.2122@nlpi064.nbdc.sbc.com>`
- **References:** `<30d67234-2965-45ed-9b66-324739333c4e@a70g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <95sk24dvp3dor42vvmao01l4vhefe7s301@4ax.com>`

```
>
>>I have a input rec coming from a customer that is defined as
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>1 LF. What would be the best way to accomplish this?


Something is wrong here.  If the record always has 129 characters, then it 
does not need to be delimited... unless - and I find this unlikely  - the 
sum of the lengths of all 16 fields is always the same.

That said, you might do better to define the input file as ..

  SELECT thefile
     organization sequential    (meaning 'record sequential' not 'line 
sequential')

FD TheFile
 01  X    PIC X (01).


Read the file a byte at a time. Buffer it up, counting commas and characters 
until you hit LineFeed. Then you have your first logical record, its length 
and comma count, and do whatever.

And no whining about "slow" ok?  You're the one with the weird set of 
conditions, so deal with it.

Personally -"If I *KNEW* the file is LF-delimited **AND** running on unix or 
could otherwise (eg compiler directive) define the record separator 
character as linefeed  I'd just call the file's organization LINE SEQUENTIAL 
and read one logical record at a time into a buffer that was both defined as 
variable lengh (RECORD CONTAINS 0 TO MANY CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON...) and 
"more than big enough to handle any malformed record."


MCM
```

---

<a id="t-63eaf947af"></a>
## Thread: Data Structure Reusability — 2 msgs

### Data Structure Reusability

- **From:** amir <ahsharif@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T00:28:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b855a6fe-c5f9-4cc3-9853-5df12929d3ae@m3g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
I want to use one Data-Structure in different places along my code.
(Microsoft & Fujitso COBOL for windows 5.0)

I write my Data-Structure in "cfSTRC.cob" as:

      * Attention! <<<DG name>>> in the COPIED place HAVE TO
SPECIFIED !!!!
      * 01 CIF-DG.
          03    CIF-Name.
               05   First-Name  pic X(20)   value ' '.
               05   Last-Name   pic X(20)   value ' '.
          03    CIF-No          pic 9(10)   value  0.
          03    CIF-Address     pic X(50)   value ' '.
          03    Filler          pic X(300).

Also wrote the code below in "cfPARAM.cob" for use in Sub-Programs.

      * Attention! <<<DG name>>> in the COPIED place HAVE TO
SPECIFIED !!!!
      * 01 CIF-DG.
          02    CIF-Count       pic 999 value 0.
          02    CIF-Data occurs 1 to 200 times
                           depending on CIF-Count.
                   copy 'cfSTRC.cob'.

Then I wrote this code in my "cfDISP.cbl" for display CIF.

       01   CIF-Data-Temp.
               copy 'cfSTRC.cob'.
       01   CIF-Array.
               copy 'cfPARAM.cob'.
       linkage section.
       01   CIF-Param.
               copy 'cfPARAM.cob'.

Compiler error message was:

    43                     depending on CIF-Count.
*   5-S******************************************
**    User-name CIF-COUNT not unique
*      CIF-COUNT
*   5-S*********
**    User-name CIF-COUNT not unique
*      CIF-COUNT
*   5-S*********
**    User-name CIF-COUNT not unique
* Checking complete - errors found

If we pre-process COPY commands in the code by hand, it's related to
CIF-Param.

Now, I have two questions:
1- What is the COBOL programming strategy for code re-usability?
Specially, in the Data-Structures arena.

2- Is there any way to handle duplications like this? I interested in
reusing my code as long as possible. Can anyone in this way?
```

#### ↳ Re: Data Structure Reusability

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T06:01:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h4hl245d93ntp32alnljmrfmiql05pv831@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b855a6fe-c5f9-4cc3-9853-5df12929d3ae@m3g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 May 2008 00:28:34 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:

>I want to use one Data-Structure in different places along my code.
>(Microsoft & Fujitso COBOL for windows 5.0)
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>reusing my code as long as possible. Can anyone in this way?

CIF-COUNT is qualified 'implicitly' under the 2002 Cobol standard. Unfortunately, that has
not been implemented yet. Maybe in another six years. Temporarily, you have to qualify it
explicitly.

          02    CIF-Count       pic 999 value 0.
          02    CIF-Data occurs 1 to 200 times
                           depending on CIF-Count in Here.
                   copy 'cfSTRC.cob'.

       01   CIF-Array.
               copy 'cfPARAM.cob' replacing Here by CIF-Array.
```

```
wrote:

>How can we refer to 10th Character of First-Name(3)... ?

Not sure what your compiler supports but you could try:

First-Name(3) (10:1)

Or you could set up a working storage field, e.g., WS-FIRST-NAME PIC
X(20).  And then:

Move First-Name(3) to WS-FIRST-NAME
Move WS-FIRST-NAME (10:1) To ...........

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"Where lipstick is concerned, the important thing is not color, 
but to accept God's final word on where your lips end."  
-- Jerry Seinfeld 
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **From:** amir <ahsharif@gmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<23a8c89d-0a07-47b4-bdab-6cecd415f2eb@r66g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<e8fe8c86-a740-4609-a396-b7f68ade4662@34g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tohl245lk03454oo2ps2bq4dkmqoqsb4ec@4ax.com> <366c6a72-33a8-4839-9538-c28e6d618c97@w34g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <0k3m24tlua8okfthbsvlsfb03f5s57s4p1@4ax.com> <9aab156d-716d-4486-9daf-b89aeccaa0b3@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <ru8m24tubva150aqvrrr1bmqn7odmcbeam@4ax.com>`

```
On May 14, 8:43 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 14 May 2008 09:45:53 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsha...@gmail.com> wrote:
> >> Move First-Name(3) to WS-FIRST-NAME
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> definitions to make the one with VALUE first.

You can use redefine as this:

01 data.
  03 filler pic x(20) value 'test1'.
  03 filler pic 9(5) value 5.
01 initialized-data-group redefines data.
  03 chars  occurs 20 times pic x.
  03 num    pic 9(5).

You can initialize your variables, as mentioned above.

Now, My problem is not to initialize whole. I want to initialize a
part of my Data-Group without affecting other parts, As mentioned
above. Exactly, In the best way (less keying, more readable, best
performance and ...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with hierarchical Data-Structure usage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T19:39:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q7vm241b0t0vubcmg6nvgicd8c1pc6sdil@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e8fe8c86-a740-4609-a396-b7f68ade4662@34g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tohl245lk03454oo2ps2bq4dkmqoqsb4ec@4ax.com> <366c6a72-33a8-4839-9538-c28e6d618c97@w34g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <0k3m24tlua8okfthbsvlsfb03f5s57s4p1@4ax.com> <9aab156d-716d-4486-9daf-b89aeccaa0b3@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <ru8m24tubva150aqvrrr1bmqn7odmcbeam@4ax.com> <23a8c89d-0a07-47b4-bdab-6cecd415f2eb@r66g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 May 2008 10:57:43 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:

>On May 14, 8:43 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Wed, 14 May 2008 09:45:53 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsha...@gmail.com> wrote:
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>  03 num    pic 9(5).

Redefines is not necessary.

01  initialized-data-group.
   03     value 'test1'.          *> word filler is unnecessary & bad style
     05   chars occurs 20 pic x.
   03  num  pic 9(5) value 5.

>You can initialize your variables, as mentioned above.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>performance and ...)

You can initialize any level having a name.

initialize CIF-Name in CIF-Temp2 (m, n)

Remember that initialize does not clear FILLERs (unnamed areas), indexes nor pointers. Nor
does it use your VALUEs. Both were fixed in the 2002 standard, but it is generally not
available.   I prefer move low-values, because it works for binary numbers and pointers as
well as pic x. The most common technique is 

move spaces to CIF-Data in CIF-Temp2 (m)
initialize CIF-Data in CIF-Temp2 (m) replacing numeric by zero

For highest speed, create an extra structure, initialize it once, move the whole thing or
the part you want to initialize. That forces you to use qualification on references to 
the base structure, which might otherwise not require qualification. Maximum speed is not
justified on every initialization, or even most. I don't recommend this technique unless
you are processing very high transaction volume. 

As a matter of style, initialization should be done INITIALLY i.e. at the beginning of a
process. You shouldn't be initializing parts of the structure in the middle of a process,
and definitely not at the end. Since you are initializing with procedural code, there
should not be a need for VALUEs in the data division except for constants that never
change. Constants can usually be handled better with 78 level. 

Indicators, one byte true/false fields with 88s, should be initialized by setting the
condition to false (Micro Focus) or by moving low-values to a group name. They should not
have data names. Setting and testing should both be done with 88s. It is bad style to
sometimes use a name and sometimes an 88.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with hierarchical Data-Structure usage

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T12:37:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oc8m24dak59igntacb71nl0s8m676iu187@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e8fe8c86-a740-4609-a396-b7f68ade4662@34g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tohl245lk03454oo2ps2bq4dkmqoqsb4ec@4ax.com> <366c6a72-33a8-4839-9538-c28e6d618c97@w34g2000prm.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 May 2008 05:17:57 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:


>We have sometimes nested arrays. What happened in this situation?
>Something like:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>How can we refer to 10th Character of First-Name(3)... ?

First-Name in CIF-Param (n3,n2,n1,3) (10:1)

You need only enough qualification to make the reference unique. Extra qualifiers, such as
CIF-Name and CIF-Data, do not hurt but are not required. 

Avoid occurs at the 01 level. Micro Focus lets you do it, other compilers do not. 
The standard says it is not allowed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with hierarchical Data-Structure usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T18:45:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<drGWj.277413$i_6.79823@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<e8fe8c86-a740-4609-a396-b7f68ade4662@34g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tohl245lk03454oo2ps2bq4dkmqoqsb4ec@4ax.com> <366c6a72-33a8-4839-9538-c28e6d618c97@w34g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <oc8m24dak59igntacb71nl0s8m676iu187@4ax.com> <f34f42eb-d8f9-49e9-a625-06dd10c0dc75@y21g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Enterprise COBOL was introduced about 30 years AFTER "OS/360" went out of 
existence.

Do you have access to the Enterprise COBOL manuals?  If not, check out:
    http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/Shelves/igy3sh40

Also, when asking how to do something with Micro Focus documentation, check out:
   http://supportline.microfocus.com/productdoc.asp
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem with hierarchical Data-Structure usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T19:54:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b72n241kaf2tbuufrolkijttbe8fr2d6m5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e8fe8c86-a740-4609-a396-b7f68ade4662@34g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tohl245lk03454oo2ps2bq4dkmqoqsb4ec@4ax.com> <366c6a72-33a8-4839-9538-c28e6d618c97@w34g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <oc8m24dak59igntacb71nl0s8m676iu187@4ax.com> <f34f42eb-d8f9-49e9-a625-06dd10c0dc75@y21g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 May 2008 11:26:33 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:

>On May 14, 8:37 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Wed, 14 May 2008 05:17:57 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsha...@gmail.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>What about Enterprise COBOL on OS/360?

Look it up here, on page 199
http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/epubs/pdf/igy3lr40.pdf

If you use occurs at the 01 level, you will not be able to initialize the structure with a
single move spaces, nor will you able to dynamically allocate it. If you pass it as a CALL
parameter, you'll have to use subscript (1), which looks like you're passing one entry
rather than the whole array.
```

## Thread: Have anyone a sample with "GetLogicalDriveStrings" — 1 msgs

### Re: Have anyone a sample with "GetLogicalDriveStrings"

- **From:** Kellie Fitton <KELLIEFITTON@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T08:11:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5fef9e8-d46f-4762-976b-9eb7286632e1@m45g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9ba01cb1-10ac-4bee-9fd7-dae80e06d93b@r66g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On May 14, 2:34 am, Bernd.Rie...@riemke-it.de wrote:
> Hi,
> I need a cobol api sample for the "GetLogicalDriveStrings"
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> I use the MF Net Express as a compiler...



Hi,

Try some thing like this:

	environment division.
	configuration section.

		special-names.
			call-convention 74 is winapi.


	working-storage section.

	01  ld-string-size		pic  9(9)   comp-5 value 512.
	01  ld-string		pic  x(512) value spaces.
	01  re-code		pic s9(9)   comp-5 value 0.


	procedure division winapi.
	100-main-section.

	call  winapi  "GetLogicalDriveStringsA" using
		by value  	ld-string-size
		by reference	ld-string
	          returning	re-code
	end-call.

Kellie.
```

```
I wrote my code as below:
'cfPARAM.cob'
----------------------
          02    CIF-Count       pic 999 value 0.
          02    CIF-Data occurs 200 times.
      *                     depending on CIF-Count.
              03    CIF-Name.
                   05   First-Name  pic X(20)   value ' '.
                   05   Last-Name   pic X(20)   value ' '.
              03    CIF-No          pic 9(10)   value  0.
              03    CIF-Address     pic X(50)   value ' '.
              03    Filler          pic X(300).
----------------------

'cdDisp.cbl'
----------------
...
linkage section.
01   CIF-Param.
               copy 'cfPARAM.cob'.
01   CIF-Idx     pic 9(4)    value 0.
       procedure division using CIF-Param CIF-Idx.
...
---------------

'cfDispT.cbl'
------------------
       01   CIF-Param.
               copy 'cfPARAM.cob'.

           call 'cfDisp.cbl' using
               by reference cif-param by reference cif-idx

I have not any problem In the compilation and linkage phase.

""Load failure (170) on file CFDISP.CBL""

Can anyone help me to solve this problem?
```

```
<snip>
> We are going to plan for migrate to IBM Mainframe environment.
> So, We have to know the language to estimate better, I think.

If using the Micro Focus compiler (not Fujitsu) and planning on developing for 
an IBM mainframe environment, make certain that you compile with the

   DIALECT(ENTCOBOL)

directive.  If you are not using a CURRENT Micro Focus Product, then use

   FLAG(ENTCOBOL),FLAGAS(S)

directives.
```

- **References:** `<3bd73949-1001-4a25-8dfc-5c02215bbeb4@x35g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <g0f4ko$fai$1@reader2.panix.com> <85416cf6-0bca-451b-ae36-7e7e52c98e12@m73g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g0f68l$db5$1@reader2.panix.com> <8703e193-6d23-4d61-97d4-69336b131d37@l42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 May 2008 10:25:27 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:

>On May 14, 8:07 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <85416cf6-0bca-451b-ae36-7e7e52c98...@m73g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am testing different capabilities of the language to make my report.

Don't overlook data type POINTER and dynamic allocation. 

Don't overlook 'nested compilations', the ability to put called programs (and functions)
in a single source file. It's better than PERFORM because each procedure has a private
working-storage. 

Try TYPEDEF instead of COPY. Try using TYPEDEF to define a date type. 

Try calling C library functions.

Avoid REDEFINES; it's a sign of amateurish programming.
```

```
COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Load Failure (170) on File ...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-15T20:15:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11ip24d6gegshe89377kuc268mkcujpomk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3bd73949-1001-4a25-8dfc-5c02215bbeb4@x35g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <g0f4ko$fai$1@reader2.panix.com> <85416cf6-0bca-451b-ae36-7e7e52c98e12@m73g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g0f68l$db5$1@reader2.panix.com> <8703e193-6d23-4d61-97d4-69336b131d37@l42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <ke3n24l1gku2553b9q0n5aj7tpad4ih8rv@4ax.com> <aa0ec136-2812-4db8-8cb8-a4a41e179be5@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 15 May 2008 04:47:03 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:

>> Don't overlook data type POINTER and dynamic allocation.
>Can you comment more?

There was no Cobol syntax for memory allocation before the 2002 standard, but it is easy
to abstract. If you have access to a C library, the abstraction is already done, just call
malloc(). There IS Cobol support for type POINTER, which is required to address the
allocated memory. You describe the external structure in linkage section, say SET ADDRESS
OF (linkage structure) TO (pointer). 

It is especially useful for linked lists and trees, which have no practical limit on their
maximum size. Contrast with arrays (defined with OCCURS), which have a arbitrary maximum. 

Here's a more mundane example. Suppose you want a function to use a default value when a
parameter is omitted. 

working-storage section.
01  default-value value 'ALL' pic x(99).

linkage section.
01  a-parameter                    pic x(99).

procedue division using a-parameter.
    if  address of a-parameter = null
        set address of a-parameter to address of default-value
    end-if

>> Try TYPEDEF instead of COPY. Try using TYPEDEF to define a date type.
>
>I will use it.

Bill Klein was correct when he said typedef is not supported by Enterprise Cobol. 

>> Try calling C library functions.
>I will try it.

C functions are especially useful for calls to the operating system API, because they are
OS-independent and  Cobol compiler-independent.  For example, getenv() reads an
environment variable the same on z/OS, Unix and Windows, the same on Micro Focus and
Enterprise Cobol. Another useful one is abort(), to abnormally terminate a batch program.
It surely is cleaner than dividing by zero, which is how mainframers once did it.

>> Avoid REDEFINES; it's a sign of amateurish programming.
>That's right. But, what about my problem with initializing some of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>What is your suggestion for this problem?

Data belongs in databases or files, not in programs.

Assuming you're using a database, there will be two types of tables: reference tables,
which are read-only, and normal data tables. Load all configuration values from a
reference table. 

Database is better than flat file because it is one less environmental thing that must be
synchronized. It reduces the probability of using client A's configuration with client B's
database. To facilitate testing configurations, use a synonym as a layer of indirection. 

Security, such as an encryption key, belongs in a separate layer, not in application
programs. If you MUST read an encryption key, don't leave it in memory in the clear,
obfuscate it by xoring an unpredictable random number.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Load Failure (170) on File ...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-16T08:43:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j77r24hb1cemgd214hgbrkiq8a8e7qj4eh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3bd73949-1001-4a25-8dfc-5c02215bbeb4@x35g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <g0f4ko$fai$1@reader2.panix.com> <85416cf6-0bca-451b-ae36-7e7e52c98e12@m73g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g0f68l$db5$1@reader2.panix.com> <8703e193-6d23-4d61-97d4-69336b131d37@l42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <ke3n24l1gku2553b9q0n5aj7tpad4ih8rv@4ax.com> <aa0ec136-2812-4db8-8cb8-a4a41e179be5@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <11ip24d6gegshe89377kuc268mkcujpomk@4ax.com>`

```
On Thu, 15 May 2008 20:15:07 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>There was no Cobol syntax for memory allocation before the 2002 standard, but it is easy
>to abstract.

At least not in the modern (TYPEDEF) style.    Sections and even
paragraph numbers were at one time used for managing memory
allocation.    

I guess in a way Java's built-in memory clean-up followed what
happened historically with CoBOL.

These aren't exact parallels (they never are), but they are similar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Load Failure (170) on File ...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-16T12:43:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vfgr241au2lcj9iqeghd20ioiuf1cvospd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3bd73949-1001-4a25-8dfc-5c02215bbeb4@x35g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <g0f4ko$fai$1@reader2.panix.com> <85416cf6-0bca-451b-ae36-7e7e52c98e12@m73g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g0f68l$db5$1@reader2.panix.com> <8703e193-6d23-4d61-97d4-69336b131d37@l42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <ke3n24l1gku2553b9q0n5aj7tpad4ih8rv@4ax.com> <aa0ec136-2812-4db8-8cb8-a4a41e179be5@w7g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <11ip24d6gegshe89377kuc268mkcujpomk@4ax.com> <j77r24hb1cemgd214hgbrkiq8a8e7qj4eh@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 16 May 2008 08:43:25 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Thu, 15 May 2008 20:15:07 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>allocation.    

Level numbers were used for managing procedural code. You need memory management for data,
not for code. OO languages, including Cobol, provide that with constructors and
destructors, often called 'new' and 'delete' or 'finalize'. 

When writing in a non-OO language such as traditional Cobol, it's nice to be able to do
the same yourself. 

>I guess in a way Java's built-in memory clean-up followed what
>happened historically with CoBOL.

Java supplies no destructors. To destroy an object, you overwrite the reference to it or
terminate the thing using it. Some time later, Java automagically destroys orphaned
objects.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Load Failure (170) on File ...

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-14T20:10:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rh2n249lupl55va2kmcq8vdhvpkiohis20@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3bd73949-1001-4a25-8dfc-5c02215bbeb4@x35g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <9b9m241c2kguu399ivb11j584ki7thh21t@4ax.com> <7bfe3b5c-54df-463b-a9fb-8a223d07bf99@d77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 14 May 2008 11:23:14 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsharif@gmail.com> wrote:

>On May 14, 8:46 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Wed, 14 May 2008 09:30:54 -0700 (PDT), amir <ahsha...@gmail.com> wrote:
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks

What is the definition of cif-idx in the calling program? Is CIF-Param defined the same
way in the calling program? Problems like this could be prevented by using call
prototypes. 

Index has a specific meaning in Cobol. It is not 9(4). That's a subscript, not an index. 
Do not call it index if it is not an index. 

By reference is the default. Specifying it on every parameter is unnecessary verbosity. 
If you feel compelled, say by reference once on the first parameter, it will apply to all
that follow. 

As a general rule, ALL parameters should be by reference unless you are calling the
Windows API or a C program. 

Do not put VALUEs in the linkage section. If the compiler lets you, the VALUEs are
meaningless. 

Copybook files are usually named .cpy
```

```
> What is the definition of cif-idx in the calling program? Is CIF-Param defined the same
> way in the calling program? Problems like this could be prevented by using call
> prototypes.

should be ok, I think.

> Index has a specific meaning in Cobol. It is not 9(4). That's a subscript, not an index.
> Do not call it index if it is not an index.
it is not an Index and your suggestion is very useful. I will do it.

> By reference is the default. Specifying it on every parameter is unnecessary verbosity.
> If you feel compelled, say by reference once on the first parameter, it will apply to all
> that follow.

> As a general rule, ALL parameters should be by reference unless you are calling the
> Windows API or a C program.

Thanks for your comments.
I will use all of them in my work.
Thanks again
```

## Thread: casting a letter into an integer — 13 msgs

### casting a letter into an integer

- **From:** mario <mmc_vw1200@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-15T07:57:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ed10b0ea-e4e8-41b5-922e-f41db3e2ce2d@k37g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi, because of an old program i have an index thats just
my_index pic X(1)
values for my_index are  0,1,2,...,A,B,....Z

now i want to transform the index into normal numeric pic 9(2)

for example
A := 10
B := 11
.
.
.
I := 18

my solution so far is not that fine:

 EVALUATE index_alphabetic
         WHEN "A"     MOVE 10     TO index_nr
         WHEN "B"     MOVE 11     TO index_nr
         WHEN "C"     MOVE 12     TO index_nr
End-evaluate

u have any better solutions for me? searching with a counter in a
perform loop


kind regards and a big thank!

mario
```

```
sequence
```

#### ↳ Re: casting a letter into an integer

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-15T12:04:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hvqo249oug5ktvo1n83rioudtuf0q4oeo3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ed10b0ea-e4e8-41b5-922e-f41db3e2ce2d@k37g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 15 May 2008 07:57:37 -0700 (PDT), mario <mmc_vw1200@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Hi, because of an old program i have an index thats just
>my_index pic X(1)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>perform loop

01  my-alphabet value '0123456789ABC ... Z'.
     05  my-letter occurs 36 pic x.
01 my-index pic 9(02).

move 1 to my-index
search my-letter varying my-index
    at end display 'Bad input ' my-input
    when my-letter (my-index) = function upper-case(my-input)
        continue
end-search
```

#### ↳ Re: casting a letter into an integer

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-15T12:27:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t2so24tijc13t6igm5858cba30rb82b86b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ed10b0ea-e4e8-41b5-922e-f41db3e2ce2d@k37g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 15 May 2008 07:57:37 -0700 (PDT), mario <mmc_vw1200@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Hi, because of an old program i have an index thats just
>my_index pic X(1)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>perform loop

move function upper-case(my-input) to my-input
evaluate my-input
    when '0' thru '9'
        compute my-index = function ord (my-input) - function ord ('0') + 1
   when 'A' thru 'I'
        compute my-index = function ord (my-input) - function ord ('A') + 11
  when 'J' thru 'R'
        compute my-index = function ord (my-input) - function ord ('J') + 20
   when 'S' thru 'Z'
        compute my-index = function ord (my-input) - function ord ('S') + 29
   when other
        display 'Bad input ' my-input
end-evaluate
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: casting a letter into an integer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-15T18:06:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vY_Wj.744496$Gl5.66436@fe02.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ed10b0ea-e4e8-41b5-922e-f41db3e2ce2d@k37g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <t2so24tijc13t6igm5858cba30rb82b86b@4ax.com>`

```
Others have already said this, but just to be absolutely clear, use of Function 
ORD to convert a letter to a number is VERY operating system/environment 
sensitive.  It certainly will NOT give you  code that can be ported from ASCII 
to EBCDIC or vice versa. If working in a Unicode environment, other issues will 
occur.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: casting a letter into an integer

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-15T15:54:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vKOdnYGXM8K5CLHVnZ2dnUVZ_gqdnZ2d@posted.mid-floridainternet>`
- **References:** `<ed10b0ea-e4e8-41b5-922e-f41db3e2ce2d@k37g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <t2so24tijc13t6igm5858cba30rb82b86b@4ax.com> <vY_Wj.744496$Gl5.66436@fe02.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:vY_Wj.744496$Gl5.66436@fe02.news.easynews.com...
> Others have already said this, but just to be absolutely clear, use of
Function
> ORD to convert a letter to a number is VERY operating system/environment
> sensitive.  It certainly will NOT give you  code that can be ported from
ASCII
> to EBCDIC or vice versa.

This should be insensitive to enviroment and, therefore, portable.

-----
      $set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"s"
       identification division.
       program-id. ltr2num.
       environment division.
       configuration section.
       object-computer. cobol-85
           sequence alphanumeric-subscript.
       special-names.
           alphabet alphanumeric-subscript "123456789"
                "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
                "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz"
           class valid-ltr "123456789"
                "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
                "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz".
       data division.
       linkage section.
       01  ltr pic x.
       01  num pic 99.
       procedure division using ltr num.
       begin.
           if ltr not valid-ltr
               move 0 to num
           else
               compute num = function ord (ltr)
           end-if.
-----

----- Test program
      $set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"s"
       identification division.
       program-id. testl2n.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  ltr pic x.
       01  num pic 99.
       01  x binary pic 9(4) value 0.
       procedure division.
       begin.
           perform
           varying x from 1 by 1
           until x > 256
               move function char (x) to ltr
               call "ltr2num" using ltr num
               if num not = 0
                   display space quote ltr "=" num quote space
                   no advancing
               end-if
           end-perform
           display space.
-----

----- Output ASCII (edited to shorten lines)
 "1=01"  "2=02"  "3=03"  "4=04"  "5=05"
 "6=06"  "7=07"  "8=08"  "9=09"  "A=10"
 "B=11"  "C=12"  "D=13"  "E=14"  "F=15"
 "G=16"  "H=17"  "I=18"  "J=19"  "K=20"
 "L=21"  "M=22"  "N=23"  "O=24"  "P=25"
 "Q=26"  "R=27"  "S=28"  "T=29"  "U=30"
 "V=31"  "W=32"  "X=33"  "Y=34"  "Z=35"
 "a=36"  "b=37"  "c=38"  "d=39"  "e=40"
 "f=41"  "g=42"  "h=43"  "i=44"  "j=45"
 "k=46"  "l=47"  "m=48"  "n=49"  "o=50"
 "p=51"  "q=52"  "r=53"  "s=54"  "t=55"
 "u=56"  "v=57"  "w=58"  "x=59"  "y=60"
 "z=61"
-----

----- Output EBCDIC (edited to shorten lines)
 "a=36"  "b=37"  "c=38"  "d=39"  "e=40"
 "f=41"  "g=42"  "h=43"  "i=44"  "j=45"
 "k=46"  "l=47"  "m=48"  "n=49"  "o=50"
 "p=51"  "q=52"  "r=53"  "s=54"  "t=55"
 "u=56"  "v=57"  "w=58"  "x=59"  "y=60"
 "z=61"  "A=10"  "B=11"  "C=12"  "D=13"
 "E=14"  "F=15"  "G=16"  "H=17"  "I=18"
 "J=19"  "K=20"  "L=21"  "M=22"  "N=23"
 "O=24"  "P=25"  "Q=26"  "R=27"  "S=28"
 "T=29"  "U=30"  "V=31"  "W=32"  "X=33"
 "Y=34"  "Z=35"  "1=01"  "2=02"  "3=03"
 "4=04"  "5=05"  "6=06"  "7=07"  "8=08"
 "9=09"
-----
```

```
environments, I don't see that this is either the quickest nor the
```

- **From:** jnjsle1@optonline.net
- **Date:** 2008-05-16T11:27:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f9ad4e2-4401-4292-8409-3bdd5a43dc26@x35g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<408bb0cb-b47e-43b4-b4f0-b604d6c36a3e@p25g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <ZLCdnRrKgbed72zanZ2dnUVZWhednZ2d@mid-floridainternet> <57qIj.23163$0o7.2366@newssvr13.news.prodigy.net> <qbi4v3lh2qh46k37lsl1v3pg0f4jppaod8@4ax.com> <B2tIj.20982$xq2.2197@newssvr21.news.prodigy.net> <65folmF1f605pU1@mid.individual.net> <ZrzIj.36801$J41.3240@newssvr14.news.prodigy.net> <88d5d239-ee24-4e3d-9e46-629b264f2cfe@d77g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <3d408bb1-de29-4505-b92d-d6a944d50d28@26g2000hsk.googlegroups.com> <hZidnbcFnu4PNLDVnZ2dnUVZ_h_inZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
On May 16, 11:34 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> jnjs...@optonline.net wrote:
> > What a mess.  Does anybody know what I have to do to get Humpty Dumpty
> > back together again?
>
> Use a smaller font. Also uses less pixels. Maybe bndwth.

Hi,

The following code may be helpful in eliminating the table lookups to
convert from printable hex to internal hex.  I used the old compiler
max for a numeric PIC [PIC 9(18)].  Converting to the lastest max
[PIC
9(31)] should not be a problem.

 
******************************************************************
       01  WS-WORK-FLDS.
 
******************************************************************
      *===>
           05  WS-PACKED               PIC  9(017)     COMP-3.
           05  WS-INTRNL-HEX           REDEFINES
               WS-PACKED               PIC  X(009).

           05  WS-UNPACKED             PIC  9(017).
           05  WS-PRNTBLE-HEX          REDEFINES
               WS-UNPACKED             PIC  9(017).

           05  COMP-INFO               PIC  X(21) VALUE
SPACES.         00000910
1     /
 
******************************************************************
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 
******************************************************************
       000-MAINLINE.
 
******************************************************************
      *===> RTN-1 COVERTS INTERNAL HEX DATA TO PRINTABLE HEX.
*
      *===>
*
      *===> RTN-2 COVERTS PRINTABLE HEX DATA TO INTERNAL HEX.
*
      *===>
*
      *===> FOR EXAMPLE THIS INTERNAL HEX DATA: X'12ACF45F'
*
      *===> PRINTS/DISPLAYS AS: >Ú4^<. TO PRINT/DISPLAY IT AS
*
      *===> >12ACF45F<, USE ROUTINE 1. TO CONVERT PRINTABLE HEX DATA
*
      *===> (E.G. 12ACF45F) TO ITS INTERNAL FORM, USE ROUTINE 2.
*
      *===>
*
      *===> I USED THE OLD PIC LIMIT OF 18 FOR DIGITS; IF YOU WANT TO
*
      *===> USE THE NEW LIMIT OF 31, USE THE ARITH(EXTEND) COMPILER
*
      *===> OPTION AND ADJUST THE PIC OF THE WORK FIELDS BELOW.
*
      *----------------------------------------------------------------
*

       000-MAIN.
           MOVE    X'12ACF45F'         TO WS-INTRNL-HEX
           DISPLAY 'INTERNAL  HEX >'   WS-INTRNL-HEX  '< '
           PERFORM RTN-1
           DISPLAY 'PRINTABLE HEX >'   WS-PRNTBLE-HEX '< '

           DISPLAY ' '

           MOVE    '12ACF45FB6980A3B'  TO WS-UNPACKED(1:17)
           DISPLAY 'PRINTABLE HEX >'   WS-PRNTBLE-HEX '< '
           PERFORM RTN-2
           DISPLAY 'INTERNAL  HEX >'   WS-INTRNL-HEX  '< '
           GOBACK
           .
       RTN-1.
 
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
      *===>  ROUTINE TO CONVERT INTERNAL HEX DATA TO PRINTABLE HEX.
 
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
           MOVE    WS-PACKED           TO WS-UNPACKED
           INSPECT WS-UNPACKED            CONVERTING
                   X'FAFBFCFDFEFF'     TO 'ABCDEF'
           .
       RTN-2.
 
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
      *===>  ROUTINE TO CONVERT PRINTABLE HEX DATA TO INTERNAL HEX.
 
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
           INSPECT WS-UNPACKED            CONVERTING
                   'ABCDEF'            TO X'FAFBFCFDFEFF'
           MOVE    WS-UNPACKED         TO WS-PACKED
           .
 
******************************************************************
      *===>  END OF PGM CODE
 
******************************************************************
```

<a id="t-f474249c83"></a>
## Thread: Memory question (follow-up to one of Amir's posts) — 1 msgs

### Memory question (follow-up to one of Amir's posts)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-19T10:26:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7BcYj.778633$Gl5.253417@fe02.news.easynews.com>`

```
Amir has mentioned that he is using the Microsoft Compiler V5.0 (rebranded Micro 
Focus compiler).

I can't remember whether that compiler did or did not have "full EBCDIC" support 
(e.g. CHARSET(EBCDIC)).

Does anyone in this group either still have that compiler or remember whether 
this was in that product or not?

Amir's claim is that he is evaluating capabilities of IBM mainframe COBOL.  To 
the extent that he isn't doing "serious" work and not trying to develop on the 
PC for same-results on IBM mainframes, EBCDIC/ASCII run-time behavior shouldn't 
be a "big deal".  However, if he actually DOES develop anything on the PC that 
is dependent on ASCII collation sequence (in general, not just the Special-Names 
items), and expects the same run-time results on an IBM mainframe, this won't 
work without full EBCDIC support.
```

---

<a id="t-28b9d9118e"></a>
## Thread: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities" — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-19T21:01:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
On May 14th in two different notes, Amir stated,

"My task is to explore the Language capabilities and report it.
I am testing different capabilities of the language to make my report."

and

"We are going to plan for migrate to IBM Mainframe environment.
So, We have to know the language to estimate better, I think."

  ***

As I have previously indicated, it is hard for me to believe that an 
organization that is large enough to already have an IBM mainframe (with a COBOL 
compiler) would assign such a task to someone with Amir's training and skill set 
(or lack therefore).  Of course it is possible that this assignment was made 
explicitly to PROVE that such a migration would be a "failure".)  It is also 
surprising (but not totally impossible) that an organization that already has an 
IBM mainframe with a COBOL compiler would be doing a NEW migration to such a 
platform.

HOWEVER,  I am willing to give him the benefit of the doubt as he has "stuck to 
his story" that this is what he is doing (and that he really has no one in the 
organization giving him this task to help him with COBOL or ISPF issues).

Therefore, this is a summary of what I think that someone with his skill-set 
should report back:

1) IBM mainframe COBOL has many if not all the capabilities of all the other 
languages that are available on that platform.  Therefore, if a migration to an 
IBM mainframe environment is expected, then the COBOL language is a reasonable 
choice.  If there is a question about migrating TO the IBM mainframe environment 
at all, then this does seem an "odd" (unusual) thing to be doing in this day and 
age.  One would need to know WHY this is being considered to give a reasonable 
answer as to whether it is a good idea or not.

2) Using IBM COBOL on the mainframe requires a LARGE number of skills.  These 
cannot be gained successfully in a piecemeal manner.  Either you need a full set 
of training or you need a large number of experienced programmers to assist 
"newbies". The skills needed include:
 - COBOL
 - JCL
 - Debugging (interactive and static) tool training

In addition, if development is to be done ON the mainframe itself, you should 
also have
 - ISPF
 - IBM utilities
 - SORT (either SyncSort or DFSORT training)
 - Additionally, you need to know what interactive and static debugging tool you 
will be using and have training for it (for example, IBM Debug Tool, Xpediter, 
Abend-Aid, DumpMaster, etc)
 - Many mainframe applications also use 3rd party tools such as SAS, EasyTrieve, 
etc

If the development of the mainframe targeted applications will be done on a PC, 
then you need to acquire current versions of appropriate (often expensive) tools 
such as
 - Micro Focus Mainframe Express
     or
 - IBM Rational Developer for zOS

In addition, most IBM mainframe applications use some or all  of the following 
which require additional training
 - CICS
 - IMS
 - DB2
 - MQSeries
Depending upon which of these are used, if development is planned to be done on 
the PC, then additional PC tools are needed.

If one wants to determine the CURRENT capabilities of IBM mainframe COBOL, you 
need a CURRENT IBM mainframe COBOL compilers.  Just some of the (not so recent 
but after IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM) enhancements that are available include
 - new OO support
 - native XML support
 - COMP-5 (or native binary) support

   ***

I am certain that others in this group could (and probably will) add to the list 
of skills needed to successfully employ COBOL in an IBM zOS environment.  The 
real questions remain as to why this is being investigated and why one with NONE 
of the required existing skills has been assigned the task of doing the 
investigation.

I remain suspicious of the claims of the reason for Amir's posts and questions 
(and "investigation").  However, if he really was assigned this task with 
minimal or no internal support, then the major thing he should report back is 
that it DOES take training, knowledge, etc to successfully migrate to an IBM 
mainframe COBOL environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-05-20T08:23:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kjn534t7a1iga2mq88limeeitg11aphqjj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Mon, 19 May 2008 21:01:29 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

Excellent post


>In addition, most IBM mainframe applications use some or all  of the following 
>which require additional training
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the PC, then additional PC tools are needed.

I don't use any of those, which doesn't change your first quoted
statement.

But as you said - such an analysis really, really needs to understand
its purpose.    Guessing about why the mainframe is being considered
won't get optimal results.
```

#### ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-21T14:40:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<69hgctF32r4dnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
This is a VERY good post , Bill.

I've watched this thread with passing interest and seen the frustration with 
Amir growing.

I'm not sure it is entirely fair to call someone an "idiot" when English is 
not their first language, and they are trying to grapple with something that 
is completely new and foreign to them, but it is also frustrating to try and 
help someone and then see they don't take your advice. (I personally believe 
this was because of lack of understanding and miscommunication, not 
intransigence on Amir's part...) Having said that, good advice was provided, 
that would be adequate for most people. The problem is that there is an 
implicit assumption that Amir has access to, and understands, terms and 
tools that are "taken as read" by people on mainframe sites.

Just because someone HAS a copy of ISPF doesn't mean they are adept with 
it...

Just because a reference is provided to an IBM text, doesn't mean a 
non-English speaker will waltz through it and enjoy the experience. (I speak 
English as a first language and I HATE reading computer reference 
manuals...IBM are not the worst, but they are still terminally boring.)

For people who have a completely different background, the world of 
mainframe COBOL is bewildering and foreign.

If you have used tools like Eclipse and Visual Studio, for example, ISPF 
seems completely unintuitive. I can see why he had problems with things 
starting in certain columns.

Most people here take the 80-column punched card image that COBOL was built 
upon, for granted. It isn't reasonable to expect other people from a more 
modern background to do so. Certainly, they will have to if they intend to 
use mainframe COBOL, and you quite properly pointed out that training is 
required for this (as for any...) environment.

I have a few other comments, interspersed below...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-20T20:03:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcf9591b-c9e8-40f8-a944-29e9e0551929@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com> <69hgctF32r4dnU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On May 21, 2:40 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:


> > - native XML support
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> really NEEDED to be in the language...

I couldn't think of a worse way of doing XML, and it won't even handle
more than a subset anyway, eg attributes.

I have just done an XML parser for another application and all the tag
names and actions are in a configuration file. The program won't need
to be changed even if a completely different XML format is called for.

Output of XML, of course, is done entirely through templates so the
program doesn't know or care whether it is XML, CSV or Postscript.

The concept that the XML structure is built into the source code is
simplay insane. Well maybe not to someone who has never seen anything
other than COBOL batch programs entirely in upper case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-21T03:42:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uSMYj.780033$us.768246@fe04.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com> <69hgctF32r4dnU1@mid.individual.net> <bcf9591b-c9e8-40f8-a944-29e9e0551929@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com>`

```
What makes you think that the CURRENT Enterprise COBOL doesn't support XML 
"attributes"? See:
   http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr40/6.2.41.5

This was added in the latest release of Enterprise COBOL.

Having said that, I do see that there are pros and cons to using the IBM native 
COBOL XML support, but from what I have heard, it has become quite popular among 
Enterprise COBOL customers.
```

```
> What makes you think that the CURRENT Enterprise COBOL doesn't support XML 
> "attributes"? See:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

XML is useful.

Integrating it into COBOL is not.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-21T17:47:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ReZYj.517830$uN4.39978@fe07.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com> <69hgctF32r4dnU1@mid.individual.net> <bcf9591b-c9e8-40f8-a944-29e9e0551929@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <uSMYj.780033$us.768246@fe04.news.easynews.com> <69idhcF32jippU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
What makes you think they were offered no other choice?  The separate XML 
"toolkit" was available BEFORE the integrated COBOL support.  Both have been 
enhanced since they were separately available. See:
  http://www-03.ibm.com/servers/eserver/zseries/software/xml/
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:bcf9591b-c9e8-40f8-a944-29e9e0551929@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com...
On May 21, 2:40 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:


> > - native XML support
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> really NEEDED to be in the language...

I couldn't think of a worse way of doing XML, and it won't even handle
more than a subset anyway, eg attributes.

I have just done an XML parser for another application and all the tag
names and actions are in a configuration file. The program won't need
to be changed even if a completely different XML format is called for.

Output of XML, of course, is done entirely through templates so the
program doesn't know or care whether it is XML, CSV or Postscript.

The concept that the XML structure is built into the source code is
simplay insane. Well maybe not to someone who has never seen anything
other than COBOL batch programs entirely in upper case.

[Pete]

Thank you for your support, Richard (sometimes I think it is just me... :-))

I agree 100% with your points above. It should never have been integrated 
and doing so was a retrograde step for COBOL.

It's quite obvious that the people (person) behind this have (has) no idea 
of what XML is about.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-21T12:54:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<508e263d-0af5-4807-841b-cb8e70529fb7@79g2000hsk.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com> <69hgctF32r4dnU1@mid.individual.net> <bcf9591b-c9e8-40f8-a944-29e9e0551929@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <69idbdF32k447U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On May 21, 10:54 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Richard" <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> and doing so was a retrograde step for COBOL.

It is as bad as creating a UI by coding it. I disliked screen section
but at least there was a drawing program to design the screen layout
(but I still didn't use it).

Interfaces should be separate from the business code, and interfaces
include UI, XML, CSV, and printed forms. There may be a case for code
to build an interface from simpler tables, or configurations, but that
should not be specific to the  business logic.

> It's quite obvious that the people (person) behind this have (has) no idea
> of what XML is about.

I am not actually a fan of XML. They say that if you have a problem
and decide to solve it using XML then you have two problems.

If I actually needed to care that is was XML then I would hate it
more, but as I do it it is just another text template that gets told
what data to stuff in it. On reading I cycle through the nodes using a
configuration table to tell the program how to deal with it, the
application doesn't care whether the data comes from XML, or a .xls or
plain text.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-21T20:17:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hph934hstvp8t7bta81oqui493afje6csa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<JUlYj.321252$i_6.156661@fe06.news.easynews.com> <69hgctF32r4dnU1@mid.individual.net> <bcf9591b-c9e8-40f8-a944-29e9e0551929@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <69idbdF32k447U1@mid.individual.net> <508e263d-0af5-4807-841b-cb8e70529fb7@79g2000hsk.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 21 May 2008 12:54:44 -0700 (PDT), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:


>If I actually needed to care that is was XML then I would hate it
>more, but as I do it it is just another text template that gets told
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>plain text.

We already have Cobol and other  programs that access data with enbedded SQL. There are
ODBC drivers that make XML look like database for input, and convert database inserts into
XML for output. That includes XML islands in HTML pages. For years we've had ODBC drivers
that make flat files, Cobol indexed files and spreadsheets look like databases. 

If you are not inclined to develop conversion functions, write your IO in SQL and use
third-party ODBC drivers to do conversions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (For Amir) - "esplorinng IBM Mainframe COBOL Language Capabilities"

```
> It is as bad as creating a UI by coding it. I disliked screen section
> but at least there was a drawing program to design the screen layout
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> should not be specific to the  business logic.

I agree with you and did not found any way for this but designing the
application architecture something like this:
+-----------------------------------------+----------------------------
+
|           Presentation Layer (UI)       | Client Configuration Tools
|
+-----------------------------------------|----------------------------
+
|        Business Layer (BPM & MQ)        |            Server
|
+-----------------------------------------|         Monitoring &
|
|     Service Dispatcher Layer (Opt.)     |        Configuration
|
+-----------------------------------------|            Tools
|
|   Service Layer (Basic Transactions )   |
|
+-----------------------------------------+----------------------------
+
| Data Access Layer (Database components) |   DBMS Maintenance Tools
|
+-----------------------------------------+----------------------------
+
I will try to do this as a simple project. maybe useful in the real
project.
Therefore, I have to make some libraries for it.
```

```
<riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>The concept that the XML structure is built into the source code is
>simplay insane. Well maybe not to someone who has never seen anything
```
