###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T11:45:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a10dmF34kpi2U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <bnvi34t7b26e2c9bp49ub9aieor2o65qj8@4ax.com> <69uat2F2u1ti1U1@mid.individual.net> <jcbk34dakcb3044qf3vd317916m3srru4b@4ax.com> <69vtbgF35ckddU2@mid.individual.net> <5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 27 May 2008 01:47:26 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Write the where clause manually, one time.

Do you mean in the Application?

I REALLY don't want to do that. As stated elsewhere, I don't want ANY SQL in 
the applications at all. All I want to see there is an invocation of the 
MOST interface, with the original COBOL ISAM access commented out above it.

It is important for future language migration to create a data access layer 
that is not embedded in COBOL.

Obviously, I can't write specific accesses into MOST manually.

The real problem is how to process the data from the interface, regarding 
the relation in the "condition" clause of the START statement, inside MOST.

I can certainly do it with dynamic SQL and MOST can build a WHERE statement 
from the data in the interface, then add that to the cursor statement and 
EXECUTE it. It will incur a performance hit, but I don't know how critical 
that is likely to be. It really depends how much of their current 
application is doing lengthy sequential processing, how often, in what 
sequences, etc. I know they use READ NEXT quite a lot for building drop down 
lists for their PowerCOBOL GUI, but these are typically fairly small 
accesses incurred only when a form is loaded and driven from that event.

The other alternative is to have the process that scans the application code 
for replacement with MOST invocations, statically build the WHERE clause and 
insert it into the application, but I don't want to do that as it violates 
my goal of having no SQL in the application. (Besides, I'd then need to put 
consequent FETCHes on the cursor into the application as well, and that gets 
ugly very quickly... :-))

When I fnally get to solve this (it should be by the end of this week), I'll 
post the code here and let the pundits pick it over... :-)

Thanks for your post, Robert.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-26T21:55:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <bnvi34t7b26e2c9bp49ub9aieor2o65qj8@4ax.com> <69uat2F2u1ti1U1@mid.individual.net> <jcbk34dakcb3044qf3vd317916m3srru4b@4ax.com> <69vtbgF35ckddU2@mid.individual.net> <5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com> <6a10dmF34kpi2U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 May 2008 11:45:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Do you mean in the Application?

In the data layer. Create a data layer program per table, containing all the SQL for that
table.

>I REALLY don't want to do that. As stated elsewhere, I don't want ANY SQL in 
>the applications at all. All I want to see there is an invocation of the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>that is not embedded in COBOL.

When I suggested writing data layers in C#, you said they HAD to be in Cobol. 

>Obviously, I can't write specific accesses into MOST manually.

I worked at one place that had all the SQL in the database. Every operation requaired two,
one to get the SQL and a second to execute it. It was a horrible solution. That's what you
will wind up with if you refuse to write SQL in data layer code. 

>The real problem is how to process the data from the interface, regarding 
>the relation in the "condition" clause of the START statement, inside MOST.

START is the easiest case. It is almost always 'key not less than :variable'. Singleton
selects have more variation. 

The where clause on UPDATEs is simple if you get in the habit of always fetching a ROWID. 
It's faster too. Same for IN followed by a subselect.

>I can certainly do it with dynamic SQL and MOST can build a WHERE statement 
>from the data in the interface, then add that to the cursor statement and 
>EXECUTE it. It will incur a performance hit, but I don't know how critical 
>that is likely to be.

Very few databases store a compiled version of embedded SQL. In most cases, it is
dynamically PREPAREd on the first execution. If you don't believe me, look in the
executable, where you'll find the original SQL as a literal with its host variables
tokenized. 

Some precompilers have an option to prepare SQL at compilation time, just to check column
names. Oracle calls the option 'semantics'. If you don't use that, you first learn about
invalid SQL at execution time. Obviously, it cannot be done for dynamically created SQL
and requires access to a test database at compilation time.

Have you ever tried putting host variables into dynamic SQL? If not, do it once to see
what's involved before you go down that road. It's a pain and it is error-prone. 

> It really depends how much of their current 
>application is doing lengthy sequential processing, how often, in what 
>sequences, etc. I know they use READ NEXT quite a lot for building drop down 
>lists for their PowerCOBOL GUI, but these are typically fairly small 
>accesses incurred only when a form is loaded and driven from that event.

Aren't most GUI controls 'bound' to a data source? In other words, load their own data. 

>The other alternative is to have the process that scans the application code 
>for replacement with MOST invocations, statically build the WHERE clause and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ugly very quickly... :-))

Trying to make the conversion 100% automated is a mistake. Convert the ISAM IO to data
layer SQL, then manually tailor where clauses. Conversion is a ONE TIME process. It
doesn't have to be done every time you compile a program. 

>When I fnally get to solve this (it should be by the end of this week), I'll 
>post the code here and let the pundits pick it over... :-)

It will be too late to change by then.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 8)_

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 27 May 2008 11:45:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> table.

That is exactly what I'm doing. It cannot contain manually written WHERE 
clauses because these are application dependent. I am looking for it to have 
a GENERAL WHERE clause structure which it can populate with data from the 
interface and EXECUTE. It is tricky, but I don't think it is impossible. 
There may have been some misunderstanding here.

>
>>I REALLY don't want to do that. As stated elsewhere, I don't want ANY SQL 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cobol.

Yes, the layer itself has to be written in COBOL, but it will be a COM 
server. As such it can be activated from ANY language. It would be embedded 
in application COBOL if I allowed SQL in the application programs.

That's why I don't want SQL in the application code. Just an invocation of 
the COM component.

It's pretty hard to explain this, and I know I haven't done a good job of 
it... sorry :-).

>
>>Obviously, I can't write specific accesses into MOST manually.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> will wind up with if you refuse to write SQL in data layer code.

No, the SQL IS in the data layer code. It is implemented as a COM component 
written in COBOL.

>
>>The real problem is how to process the data from the interface, regarding
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> selects have more variation.

Yes, I'm realizing that... :-) I'm hoping to get a general mechanism that 
can build ANY WHERE clause, whether it comes from a START condition or a 
discrete ISAM access. It is certainly keeping me off the streets...:-)
>
> The where clause on UPDATEs is simple if you get in the habit of always 
> fetching a ROWID.
> It's faster too. Same for IN followed by a subselect.

Is ROWID a feature of the RDBMS or is it a column you have defined? Not 
familiar with this.
>
>>I can certainly do it with dynamic SQL and MOST can build a WHERE 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> tokenized.

Robert, why would I not believe you?  I asked for help; it would be a bit 
stupid to disbelieve help being offered when it comes from a knowledgeable 
source.
>
> Some precompilers have an option to prepare SQL at compilation time, just 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Have you ever tried putting host variables into dynamic SQL?
No, I don't think so. I have limited experience with dynamic SQL in general, 
but I am learning rapidly... :-)


>If not, do it once to see
> what's involved before you go down that road. It's a pain and it is 
> error-prone.
>
I'll take your word for it. What alternative would you propose?

>> It really depends how much of their current
>>application is doing lengthy sequential processing, how often, in what
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> their own data.

No.  These days, in languages other than COBOL, they certainly are, but in 
PowerCOBOL I don't think there was a facility to bind to a datasource. (I 
could be wrong...) All of the PowerCOBOL I've seen doesn't use bound 
datasources. Anyway, it doesn't matter; I have inspected the code and they 
don't use bound datasources...

>
>>The other alternative is to have the process that scans the application 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Trying to make the conversion 100% automated is a mistake.

Why?  I've always found that trying to do something is a positive step 
towards achieving it. Certainly, it won't happen if I don't try.


>Convert the ISAM IO to data
> layer SQL, then manually tailor where clauses.

If I do that I'd need to tailor manually every single COM server, and change 
it manually for every application program that uses that particular ISAM 
file, before inserting invokes into that particular application.  There has 
to be a better way. If the COM server is generalised to handle whatever the 
application program requires, then I don't need to do that. The only part 
that is problematic, is the conditions on things like singletons and START. 
SQL provides the capability to build a query at run time and execute it. I 
believe the application can pass enough information through the interface to 
allow the COM server to do that.


> Conversion is a ONE TIME process. It
> doesn't have to be done every time you compile a program.

I think there may be confusion here between what is application code and 
what is data layer code. Each application will be "converted" by having its 
current ISAM access changed into INVOKES of the COM server. Yes, that SHOULD 
be a one time process.  I plan to automate the process of replacing the ISAM 
verbs in the application with code that will build the interface and invoke 
the COM server. The tool that does this can have enough "intelligence" to 
parse the conditions and decompose them to a form that can be passed through 
the interface.

>
>>When I fnally get to solve this (it should be by the end of this week), 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It will be too late to change by then.

Robert, it is NEVER too late to change something that is wrong...:-)

Thanks for your post. I do consider what you write.

Pete.
```

The concept here one might wish to consider, Mr Dashwood, is 'the law of 
diminishing returns'.  Yes, 100% conversion of a given system's 
idiosyncracies might be possible... but this might not occur within the 
project's timeline.

'On time, on spec, on budget... pick two'.

[snip]

>> It will be too late to change by then.
>
>Robert, it is NEVER too late to change something that is wrong...:-)

From http://www.bartleby.com/66/61/22261.html

--begin quoted text:

The Moving Finger writes, and having writ,
Moves on; nor all your Piety nor Wit
Shall lure it back to cancel half a line,
Nor all your Tears wash out a Word of it. 

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T08:20:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l80o34ttp1n7q9bch9na06r6f3h45s020d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <bnvi34t7b26e2c9bp49ub9aieor2o65qj8@4ax.com> <69uat2F2u1ti1U1@mid.individual.net> <jcbk34dakcb3044qf3vd317916m3srru4b@4ax.com> <69vtbgF35ckddU2@mid.individual.net> <5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com> <6a10dmF34kpi2U1@mid.individual.net> <dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com> <6a29l1F31e74uU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 May 2008 23:29:35 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
>news:dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>There may have been some misunderstanding here.

It's not that simple. The statement will say

EXECUTE :handle USING :host-variable1, host-variable2, ...

The number of host variable pointers, which is fixed at compilation time, must equal the
number of bind variable tokens in your dynamic SQL. 

That's why I recommended doing it once to see what's involved. 

There IS a way to modify the bind variable list at execution time. It is the DESCRIBE
verb, which is in guru territory. Your frustration trigger will be pulled when you
discover DESCRIBE changes the number of arguments and their names, but not their types.
You'll wind up converting everything to and from strings (varchars) or much worse,
enbedding data in the SQL 'just to get it working'.

The practical solution is a  separate entry point (method) for each SQL statement.
Usually, the only difference is the hard coded WHERE clause. 


>Yes, the layer itself has to be written in COBOL, but it will be a COM 
>server. As such it can be activated from ANY language. It would be embedded 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the COM component.

That's the classical three layer client/server model. 

Don't assume the data layer must execute on the same machine as the application. It is
semi-common for the invocation to send a message through middleware. 


>> The where clause on UPDATEs is simple if you get in the habit of always 
>> fetching a ROWID.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>familiar with this.

It's a database feature. ROWID is your friend.

>> Have you ever tried putting host variables into dynamic SQL?
>No, I don't think so. I have limited experience with dynamic SQL in general, 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'll take your word for it. What alternative would you propose?

Hard code WHERE clauses. 

>>Convert the ISAM IO to data
>> layer SQL, then manually tailor where clauses.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>allow the COM server to do that.

If you go with dynamic SQL, you'd better master SQL debugging tools, because you'll be
spending a lot of time using them. A handy one that's often overlooked is ODBC tracing. 

By all means, get a copy of TOAD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>

- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <bnvi34t7b26e2c9bp49ub9aieor2o65qj8@4ax.com> <69uat2F2u1ti1U1@mid.individual.net> <jcbk34dakcb3044qf3vd317916m3srru4b@4ax.com> <69vtbgF35ckddU2@mid.individual.net> <5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com> <6a10dmF34kpi2U1@mid.individual.net> <dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com> <6a29l1F31e74uU1@mid.individual.net> <l80o34ttp1n7q9bch9na06r6f3h45s020d@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:l80o34ttp1n7q9bch9na06r6f3h45s020d@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 27 May 2008 23:29:35 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> number of bind variable tokens in your dynamic SQL.

Suppose I PREPARE a statement that references 1 or more Host variables and 
then simply EXECUTE that without passing it any parameters?

Example:

instead of: PREPAREing a parameterized statement like:

SELECT CustName FROM Customers WHERE CustID = ?

...and executing it as:    EXECUTE handle USING  "10001"

I was to say:

SELECT CustName FROM Customers WHERE CustID = :HVCustID

...then...

Move "10001" to HVCustID
EXECUTE handle

Can I do that? (I know I could set it up and try it, but I'm hoping to save 
some time and also get your opinion on the approach taken.)

If I can legally do this and PREPARE late binds my statement I wouldn't need 
to worry about parameter lists at all.



>
> That's why I recommended doing it once to see what's involved.
>

I'm still building infrastructure, but I will do this as you suggest, when I 
get to the nitty-gritty.

> There IS a way to modify the bind variable list at execution time. It is 
> the DESCRIBE
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> enbedding data in the SQL 'just to get it working'.

I'll try and avoid that and keep my statements simple.
>
> The practical solution is a  separate entry point (method) for each SQL 
> statement.
> Usually, the only difference is the hard coded WHERE clause.
>

Interesting. Thanks.
>
>>Yes, the layer itself has to be written in COBOL, but it will be a COM
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's the classical three layer client/server model.

Yes. I have been advocating and using it for nearly two decades now... :-)
>
> Don't assume the data layer must execute on the same machine as the 
> application. It is
> semi-common for the invocation to send a message through middleware.

I don't make assumptions and I have designed and developed distributed 
applications. I was using distributed COM+ when everyone was saying how 
vulnerable it was and how it could never work. It worked fine and we were 
never hacked. (Since then, things have moved on and .NET is the way to go 
for Win Users, now. This current exercise is a step in that direction.

>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's a database feature. ROWID is your friend.

Thanks. I've never used it but will check it out and do so.
>
>>> Have you ever tried putting host variables into dynamic SQL?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hard code WHERE clauses.

I'm coming to the conclusion that that won't be an option, but I haven't 
decided yet. Until I actually get to coding it and trying it, I'll keep an 
open mind.

I'm still not clear on where this hard code would be and how it would work. 
You say in the data layer, but I don't see how I can hard code clauses that 
I don't know about until run time. I don't like this scenario and I don't 
want to have to manually amend every MOST component before I run an 
application against it. I may have misunderstood what you're suggesting or 
you may not understand the approach I am taking. Either way, I can't 
manually amend MOST.
>
>>>Convert the ISAM IO to data
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> ODBC tracing.

Yes, I have used ODBC tracing. It is a good tool, especially when the ODBC 
driver is dodgy... :-) I spent a couple of days tracing SQL once and then 
found the ODBC driver needed an update. Did it and everything worked fine... 
:-)

Thanks for your post, Robert.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T23:19:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j8ip341si77r6t66q7suk382081bbpf28v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <bnvi34t7b26e2c9bp49ub9aieor2o65qj8@4ax.com> <69uat2F2u1ti1U1@mid.individual.net> <jcbk34dakcb3044qf3vd317916m3srru4b@4ax.com> <69vtbgF35ckddU2@mid.individual.net> <5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com> <6a10dmF34kpi2U1@mid.individual.net> <dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com> <6a29l1F31e74uU1@mid.individual.net> <l80o34ttp1n7q9bch9na06r6f3h45s020d@4ax.com> <6a3lakF35vku6U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 28 May 2008 11:55:01 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
>some time and also get your opinion on the approach taken.)

You can't do that. HVCustID is in the client's memory. The server can't access the
client's memory. In the case of statically compiled SQL, the preprocesor tokenizes the
reference, called a bind variable, and passes the value in a message, or receives a value
from the server and copies it into your host variable. In the case of dynamic SQL, you
explicitly code dummy words as bind variable placekeepers, put the host variables on the
other side of USING/INTO. In both cases, the server never sees the host variable names,
only the placekeeper bind variables. 

PREPARE xyz FROM 'SELECT CustName FROM Customers WHERE CustID = :BV1'

EXECUTE xyz USING  :HVCustID INTO :HVCustName

The number of bind variables must equal the number of USING host variables. The number of
columns in the select list must match the number of INTO host variables. 

As I said, there is a way to control the number of variables at execution time using
DESCRIBE. My guess is that 1 programmer in 100 has ever used DESCRIBE. 

>If I can legally do this and PREPARE late binds my statement I wouldn't need 
>to worry about parameter lists at all.

You wouldn't have to worry about parameter lists for USING if you replaced :BV1 with the
value in HVCustID, written as a literal. But you'd have to PREPARE (compile) the statement
every time you execute it. That is slow and abusive. It doesn't solve the problem in the
other direction, the INTO. 

>> That's why I recommended doing it once to see what's involved.
>>
>
>I'm still building infrastructure, but I will do this as you suggest, when I 
>get to the nitty-gritty.

If you don't consider it now, you won't appreciate that you're stepping into a mine field
with dynamic SQL. 

I once used dynamic SQL when I had to load and unload several hundred tables. I read the
data dictionary, the REAL one in the database not my own home-grown version, and
dynamically built the SQL to load or unload ANY table. Why did I write my own when Oracle
supplies several such tools? Because it was an Informix database. Because the program
could read Cobol indexed files as well as flat files. Mostly, because it was fun. :) 

I mention that to show, even though I have the skill to do it, I wouldn't use dynamic SQL
if I were in your position. 

>> There IS a way to modify the bind variable list at execution time. It is 
>> the DESCRIBE
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
>open mind.

It works better if you design first, code second. In the Good Old Days, we caught a lot of
criticism for trying to do them ambivalently. :)

>I'm still not clear on where this hard code would be and how it would work. 
>You say in the data layer, but I don't see how I can hard code clauses that 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>manually amend MOST.

Use MOST to write MOST (appropriate name) of the data layer. If you think you can generate
the WHERE clause, give it a try. Then hand tailor the WHERE clauses in the data layers.

Keep in mind, the Cobol ISAM is all static. There's no such thing as dynamic ISAM. I
really doubt your applications are building FCDs on the fly.

I see MOST as a precompiler, not a runtime thing.

>>>>Convert the ISAM IO to data
>>>> layer SQL, then manually tailor where clauses.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>file, before inserting invokes into that particular application. 

Yep. That's what we do here an every other place I've worked. We have hundreds of data
layers, each containing about ten functions. 

We have another layer, called services, that perform common (multi-application) functions
involving multiple tables. For instance, creating a new customer hits 20-40 tables.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-29T12:24:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a6be8F35ipl0U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <bnvi34t7b26e2c9bp49ub9aieor2o65qj8@4ax.com> <69uat2F2u1ti1U1@mid.individual.net> <jcbk34dakcb3044qf3vd317916m3srru4b@4ax.com> <69vtbgF35ckddU2@mid.individual.net> <5nvl34pdmegh0jld4fnl3t0e80phj5k5an@4ax.com> <6a10dmF34kpi2U1@mid.individual.net> <dbrm34h6fnvtl296cbteg5d6j76e3cl3a0@4ax.com> <6a29l1F31e74uU1@mid.individual.net> <l80o34ttp1n7q9bch9na06r6f3h45s020d@4ax.com> <6a3lakF35vku6U1@mid.individual.net> <j8ip341si77r6t66q7suk382081bbpf28v@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:j8ip341si77r6t66q7suk382081bbpf28v@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 28 May 2008 11:55:01 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" 
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
> You can't do that. HVCustID is in the client's memory.

Not sure what you mean by "Client", this would be in the MOST server and the 
HV would have been declared in the program doing the PREPARE.

Nevertheless, I'll take it that that's non-viable. Thanks.


>The server can't access the
> client's memory. In the case of statically compiled SQL, the preprocesor 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> only the placekeeper bind variables.

OK, thanks for the clarification.

>
> PREPARE xyz FROM 'SELECT CustName FROM Customers WHERE CustID = :BV1'
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> other direction, the INTO.

Noted. Thanks.
>
>>> That's why I recommended doing it once to see what's involved.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have negotiated a few minefields in my time... I'm still walking :-)

> I once used dynamic SQL when I had to load and unload several hundred 
> tables. I read the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> was fun. :)

To me, that is usually reason enough to do anything :-)

>
> I mention that to show, even though I have the skill to do it, I wouldn't 
> use dynamic SQL
> if I were in your position.

Yes, I understand your point, Robert. I just can't do this with manual 
amendments. I'd rather not do it at all. We have computer technology and it 
is good. I want to use it.

>
>>> There IS a way to modify the bind variable list at execution time. It is
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
> criticism for trying to do them ambivalently. :)

Approaches to problem solving tend to be very subjective things. What works 
well for one person doesn't work so well for another. I have tried many 
approaches over the years, including "designing" and "evolving", and 
sometimes just the "flash of insight" that comes after a night's sleep when 
the subconscious has been beavering away without my knowledge... :-) 
Sometimes coding first helps to reveal technical problems with a design, or 
suggest an approach that will mean less coding, sometimes it is all 
pointless and needs to be redone because the overall concept wasn't grasped 
properly and designed first.

I use combinations of all of the above, although I have leaned towards 
"evolving" systems during the last decade. This means having a clear concept 
of "HOW" it can be done (not necessarily to the lowest level of detail) and 
knowing what the goals and deliverables are, then iteratively coding a 
solution towards that.

>
>>I'm still not clear on where this hard code would be and how it would 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> data layers.

Certainly MOST will contain ALL of the SQL code. I intend to try the dynamic 
SQL approach. You may be right and I will end up making significant manual 
"adjustments" but I have to try... :-)
>
> Keep in mind, the Cobol ISAM is all static. There's no such thing as 
> dynamic ISAM. I
> really doubt your applications are building FCDs on the fly.

No, of course they are not.

>
> I see MOST as a precompiler, not a runtime thing.
>

No, the "precompiler"part is inserting the INVOKEs of MOST into the existing 
app code. MOST is a compiled COM server that uses the data in the interface 
to provide a service to the application programs.

It ISN'T a one time thing, but the action of amending the apps to INVOKE it, 
should be. This pre-compile step will be part of my existing Migration 
Toolset and is being written in C#.

>>>>>Convert the ISAM IO to data
>>>>> layer SQL, then manually tailor where clauses.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> layers, each containing about ten functions.

I reckon you guys haven't tried hard enough... :-)

Seriously, I value the experience you have and the information you have 
provided, Robert..  Nevertheless, I need to have a go at this and I believe 
I can get a solution. If I crash and burn, you can tell me "I told you so" 
and no hard feelings... :-)


>
> We have another layer, called services, that perform common 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think you are operating on a large commercial scale where you are probably 
bound by considerations that I'm not. My solution is aimed at SMEs who don't 
have loads of money and don't have HUGE (say, more than 500 ISAM files) 
requirements either.

Thanks for your post.

Pete.
```

```
On Wed, 28 May 2008 11:55:01 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
>to worry about parameter lists at all.
Pete,

When using dynamic SQL you almost always need to PREPARE.
You also need to use DESCRIBE in some situations, but NOT in all of
them.
small example below. Take in consideration that dependign on how you
go on the dynamic sql route, you will not be able to do it exactly as
follows.
           move "SELECT TABNAME FROM SYSCAT.TABLES
      -       " ORDER BY 1
      -       " WHERE TABNAME <> ?" to st.
           EXEC SQL PREPARE s1 FROM :st END-EXEC. 
           EXEC SQL DECLARE c1 CURSOR FOR s1 END-EXEC. 
           move "STAFF" to parm-var.
           EXEC SQL OPEN c1 USING :parm-var END-EXEC. 
           perform Fetch-Loop thru End-Fetch-Loop
              until SQLCODE not equal 0.

           EXEC SQL CLOSE c1 END-EXEC. 
       Fetch-Loop Section.
           EXEC SQL FETCH c1 INTO :table-name END-EXEC.            if
SQLCODE not equal 0
              go to End-Fetch-Loop.
           display "TABLE = ", table-name.
       End-Fetch-Loop. exit.

The above SQL does not need DESCRIBE to work.



At this stage i would like to know what database you will be using.
Reason behind this is to see if you are restricted by the internal
precompiler from Fujitsu(e.g. if you will be using SQL Server) or if
you can use the specific database precompiler.

I am looking in my programs for a sample that will cover most of what
you need to do, and will post (and send by email) to you today most
likely.


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

On Thu, 29 May 2008 23:50:59 +1200, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>didn't explain it either)

It means the first column in the result set. One could alternatively use its name TABNAME.
They allow it because of UNIONs (intersect, minus, etc.), where the first columns in the
select lists might have different names. It's not necessary because the result set uses
names from the first select in the union. The same notation can be used for GROUP BY. 

I'm surprised you haven't seen this because it is frequently used (by two finger typists).

>>>>      -       " WHERE TABNAME <> ?" to st.
>>>>           EXEC SQL PREPARE s1 FROM :st END-EXEC.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>That's what I meant by my comment (I realize it was ambiguous).

Declare is a statement to the precompiler; it is not sent to the server. It defines a
local handle for the cursor. PREPARE creates a remote handle to the compiled select. 
OPEN sends either the select, in the case of static, or its remote handle, in the case of
dynamic.
```

Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> Take in consideration that dependign on how you

>"ORDER BY 1" ?   I don't understand this.

<http://www.google.com/search?sourceid=navclient&ie=UTF-8&rls=GGLJ,GGLJ:2006-39,GGLJ:en&q=%22order+by+1%22>

(It is not the way I would, when given the choice, code things... but I 
seem to recall seeing something like this in code slapped together by some 
Peat, Marwick folks around 1987 or so.  ORDER BY (numeric) is sorting on 
the column(s) SELECTed in numeric sequence.  From 
http://www.techonthenet.com/sql/order_by.php :

--begin quoted text:

You can also sort by relative position in the result set, where the first 
field in the result set is 1. The next field is 2, and so on.

SELECT supplier_city
FROM suppliers
WHERE supplier_name = 'IBM'
ORDER BY 1 DESC;

This would return all records sorted by the supplier_city field in 
descending order, since the supplier_city field is in position #1 in the 
result set.

--end quoted text)

DD
```

On Fri, 30 May 2008 00:42:31 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.
Just to add to Doc's explanation.

There are some cases where it is needed to use positional instead of
named fields.

For example.

select client, count(client) as nr_of_orders
from invoices
group by client
order by nr_of_orders descending.


The above will fail in almost all SQL implementations. 

2 solutions here.
1 - positional order

select client, count(client) as nr_of_orders
from invoices
group by client
order by 2 descending.

3- temporary table
select * from (
select client, count(client) as nr_of_orders
from invoices
group by client) z (or as z depending on db engine)
order by nr_of_orders descending.


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

Hey Pete


There are some very good points in this discussion.

I’ll tell you what, send me a small program and all the copy books to
compile it and I’ll send you the sql, vb.net (I don’t use c#),  and
cobol code needed to run this application in sql.

A few things that you should know.
•	In a trade off of disk size vs speed I chose speed.
•	Each program has a single data connection. If you have dozens of
programs with dozen of files (tables)  maintaining a connection of
each becomes a marshaling issue.
•	Date time fields are pic 9(8)v9(9). 9(8)=date 9(9)=time
•	I don’t use locks, they are stripped by the converter.
•	I don’t use comp fields, they are stripped by the converter.
•	I don’t store single quotes in the data base.
•	Leading spaces in alpha fields.  I don’t  have a problem if I’m
loading the data through a cobol program but if I use a text dirver or
sql bulk load utility the data tends to shift.
•	I don’t use sorts. On the system where most of my cobol code was
developed, writing to an indexed file and reading the file back was
faster than a sort. So no sorts.
•	I write in Cobol, VB and SQL fairly interchangeably. So if the
customer master file has a prime key of CST-ACCOUNT the sql table name
is CST_ACCOUNT. So if I’m researching an issue I can find the problem
regardless of the language used.
•	I’m still using Visual Studio 2003, mainly so I don’t have to rebuy
the Fujitsu compiler.


I would prefer a program that starts a program with a multi part
primary key. Something like an invoice header followed by an invoice
detail read for example, or that control break program you were
discussing the other day. The program should use indexed files and
start, read, etc  no “exec sql”.  If you want me to generate the data
conversion programs let me know.

Let me know the name of the default data base you want to use. I am
reworking the encryption routines that I use to pass user, password
etc. so I will probably strip that code. I will code it for a trusted
connection to the data base you choose.

I am considering build a web site where someone could upload selects,
fd, and programs and have them converted to use sql. It is still in
the planning stage. Other projects have priority.  My conversion
programs are mostly cobol running against text files. Changing them to
run against a sql table is a minor change, changing so that each user
has their own conversion rules, is not.
For this reason I don’t want the vb or sql code posted to the
internet. The cobol code I don’t care about.

I am looking for feedback to improve my code and find the issues that
I overlooked.
 Let me know what you think.

Tom
TLeaders    gmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-26T22:42:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sun, 25 May 2008 14:41:09 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I'm currently looking at automated code conversion for migrating COBOL 
>applications that use Indexed files to RDB.
snip
>
>All of the singleton type things are pretty easy, but the sequential 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>conditions.  Any thoughts?
As I said on another post, it is hard to give a accurate response
without know EXACTLY how this application works.

Ill give you one example.

Table A with index as follows.
company
accounting_year
accounting_month
product_code
sequence_number

Some of your programs may access the data with a START where they
populate the first 3 fields only. e.g. return all records within a
particular company/year/month.
your SQL where clause would be
where company = :w-company
and accounting_year = :w-year
and accounting_month = :w-month

Easy enough to code staticaly.

Other programs would instead also require the product code.
your SQL where clause would then be
where company = :w-company
and accounting_year = :w-year
and accounting_month = :w-month
and product_code = :w-product-code

Easy again.

Now we can try and do a single SQL that would allow for BOTH
situations.

where company = :w-company
and accounting_year = :w-year
and accounting_month = :w-month
and (product_code = :w-product-code or 0=:w-product-ind)

On this situation, programs that do NOT require the product code to be
a specific one would set w-product-ind to "0". The others would set it
to "1", and supply the product code.
This can eventually be set by the DB IO module DEPENDING on whether
the content of the variable product_code allows for a default value
that would never be a valid content of the table. e.g. spaces on the
case of a alphanumeric field.
It is tricky depending on your current data. But it is a possibility.

But it has a adverse effect.

While my second example will use 4 columns to access the index and
determine which records to retrieve, the 3rd one will only use the
first 3, even though product_code will be a sargable predicate. This
obviously depends on the database engine you use, and whether the SQL
is bound at runtime or at compile time, and if at compile time,
whether variables are optimized at runtime or not. (rmdb dependent).

I think you get the general idea here. Main thing is to identify
,first of all, what are the possible START conditions for each current
index on each indexed file.
Once this is done, you will have a good idea of how many cursors you
will need PER index. It may be the case that you will need to create
more than 1 per index in order to keep performance acceptable (and
don't tell me you are not considering it at the moment. You will
regret it if you do not).

If you need to go down the route of dynamic SQL, do so, BUT, and this
is a big BUT, code it in a way that you build your SQL using parameter
markers, and then populate them within the IO Module. DO NOT build the
straight SQL using the supplied values.
Reason for this is that each DBMS manager will keep a pool of
dynamically prepared statements in memory. And it will reuse them,
along with the respective access path, whenever any other process
decides to use the same SQL. This pool is memory limited, and if it
increases a lot, you will have SEVERE performance problems, including
the possibility of your database hanging.


>
>In every case MOST will return all fields (SELECT *) so the effect is the 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Regarding this bit, just one suggestion.
Code your SELECT by mentioning ALL the fields on it.
not as a SELECT *.


Regarding another point you mentioned on one of your posts regarding
validating if A start does return any records.
You mentioned that you intended to do a open cursor, fetch next to
determine this.
You may find that it will perform better if you code as follows.
exec sql
select coalesce(max(first_key_field),'invalid_key') into :host_var
from tblx where first_key_field = :key_field and ....
end-exec.
Under normal conditions this sql will always return 0 on SQLCODE.
If not then the error is not an indication that the key does not
exist, but rather another error on the table/database, or eventually a
-911 (record locked timeout on DB2).

If sqlcode is zero, then host_var will either contain 'invalid_key' as
text, or it will contain the value of the first key field of that
table. You are really only interested to know if it contains
invalid_key or not, and act accordingly.

Just a bit more food for your thoughts.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

>> ,first of all, what are the possible START conditions for each current
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T08:46:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajen345knduqo83ouns311uddnkia96b02@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <6a11phF34q1kvU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 May 2008 12:09:20 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>news:8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com...
snip
>>
>> If you need to go down the route of dynamic SQL, do so, BUT, and this
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>That sounds like poor RDBMS management...

This is basic database manager stuff.
SQL Server, SYBASE, ORACLE, DB2 all do it the same way.
Dynamic SQL buffer pool size is limited by memory in all cases. I've
seen SQL Server hang quite often cause of this, and so has Oracle in
some cases. DB2 havent seen it (yet).

In all 3 I have seen huge performance problems, and all caused because
of dynamic sql statements as follows.

select id, name, department .... from user_table where name = 'patty'
select id, name, department .... from user_table where name = 'john'
select id, name, department .... from user_table where name = 'robert'
select id, name, department .... from user_table where name = 'ian'

Above SQL is basically the same in all cases. But it was coded as
string with the name hardcoded, instead of using a prepared statement
with parameter marks.

As such ALL RDMBs will catch each individual statement as different.
I remember a particular client, that at one stage had over 14000
identical statements on cache. It was the original sign-on to the
database by the user to get user info as per above. a lot more fields
though.


ps. I do this for a living just in case anyone wonders.


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-26T22:52:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 May 2008 22:42:39 +0100, Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
wrote:

>On Sun, 25 May 2008 14:41:09 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
>to "1", and supply the product code.

where company = :w-company
and accounting_year = :w-year
and accounting_month = :w-month
and product_code = 
    case when :w-product-code > ' ' then :w-product-code
            else   product_code
   end

This will not work if product_code in the database is null.

>This can eventually be set by the DB IO module DEPENDING on whether
>the content of the variable product_code allows for a default value
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>the possibility of your database hanging.

I second that recommendation. If you put variables in SQL as literals, every SQL you
execute will cause a compilation. 

>>In every case MOST will return all fields (SELECT *) so the effect is the 
>>same as COBOL record processing. (This is not intended to be an end point; 
>>it is a start to get the existing system running against a RDB as 
>>datasource, rather than against indexed files, and without changing the 
>>existing logic.)

>Regarding this bit, just one suggestion.
>Code your SELECT by mentioning ALL the fields on it.
>not as a SELECT *.

I second that, as well. SELECT * is a nightmare when adding a column.

>Regarding another point you mentioned on one of your posts regarding
>validating if A start does return any records.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>invalid_key or not, and act accordingly.

If there are 10,000 rows containing the selected first_key_field (e.g. company), you are
telling it to read all 10,000. A better way is:

select null from table where first_key_field = :key_field and rownum = 1

This will return SQLCODE = -100 (no data found) if no rows match.

In a where clause, EXISTS does the same -- it exits on the first matching row:

WHERE EXISTS (select null from table where first_key_field = :key_field)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T08:34:51+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g7cn34hu5v1jo5rio2ve86pftn2ef7j4rp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 May 2008 22:52:47 -0500, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>On Mon, 26 May 2008 22:42:39 +0100, Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
>wrote:
>
snip
>
>>Regarding another point you mentioned on one of your posts regarding
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Robert. you are wrong on the assumption that my sql will return all
rows.

Had you looked properly at it, you would have noticed the use of
MAX(first_key_field). This associated with the fact that we are
dealing with a case where every index on the COBOL files will have its
corresponding index on the database, means that my sql will process a
single row of the index. This is true in all main RDBMS.
For example on SQL Server the above SQL will translate immediately by
the engine to a TOP function with index seek, no data access.
DB2 will translate to a "one fetch index access as MAX used", and both
will perform under 1 hundredth of a second

also rownum = x is database dependent. my code isnt.


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T07:31:50-05:00

more descriptive. My Cobol background is showing.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** razor <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T06:04:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com> <g7cn34hu5v1jo5rio2ve86pftn2ef7j4rp@4ax.com> <p9vn34tortqlb8ie1g789qjdcf255kvd98@4ax.com>`

```
Hi Pete

Really good discussion this, and most useful to me. I've tried a bit
of this in the past but gave up when I got busy with 'real work' for
which I was to get paid for. I had a couple of problems that stopped
me in my tracks and I could not get answers to.

1. When we typically read a Customer Record in COBOL, we read with
lock and present the details to the user, allow them to amend, rewrite
and then unlock. How can we do this as an equivalent within a COBOL
program. Your routines would convert a very simple READ to a very
simple SQL statement. The user may take 10 mins to change the details
and the thing I don't understand is how we can ensure the record (row)
is locked and another user cannot change the data. I do admit  that my
SQL knowledge is limited and I may be missing something here.

2. My other problem was where I was trying to replicate the
'scrolling' of my screens. All of my screens have nine rows that allow
users to page up and down through a file (or between certain
parameters if dealing with say, an order lines file for order number
nnn). I have GUI and non-GUI versions of this and I don't bind these
objects to a data source as I want to retain the control within the
COBOL program. I use the cursors as you very first suggested with a
descending option when I need it and i also used a 'LIMIT 10' on each
one to only get 9 records. Anyway, when I came to try and replicate
the following code, I got problems:

FD  ORDER-LINES.
01  ORDERL-REC.
  03 ORDERL-KEY.
    05  ORDERL-NO         PIC 9(7).
    05  ORDERL-PRODUCT    PIC 9(6).
  03  ORDERL-QTY          PIC 9(7).

..
..
..
  MOVE ORDERH-NO TO ORDERL-NO.
  MOVE WS-LAST-PRODUCT TO ORDERL-PRODUCT
  START ORDER-LINES KEY IS NOT LESS THAN ORDERL-KEY

  PERFORM 9 TIMES
      READ
         BLAH, BLAH
  END-PERFORM


Its a couple of years ago now but as I recall I could only pass one
parameter from the COBOL program to the dynamic part of the SQL code.
The way I tried to do it was by having an additional 13 character text
field as the key in the DB (order no and product code) and
manipulating this as a text string. This was obviously very wrong and
very clunky. How should I have done this Pete?

Cheers

Ian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-27T12:39:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com> <g7cn34hu5v1jo5rio2ve86pftn2ef7j4rp@4ax.com> <p9vn34tortqlb8ie1g789qjdcf255kvd98@4ax.com> <810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 27 May 2008 06:04:08 -0700 (PDT), razor <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote:

>Hi Pete
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>SQL knowledge is limited and I may be missing something here.

You never want to lock users out. Having a customer row shouldn't stop others from
browsing the customer table. Let them have a dirty read. 

If you don't want to be bothered understanding database locks, here's a simple way to
detect collisions that will work on any database.

select row_version, name, address ... from customers ...

-- long delay for update and lunch --

update customers
    set row_version = row_version + 1,
         name = :hv-name, ....
   where row_version = :hv-row-varsion

In the unlikely event you get an error, someone else updated the row while your user was
out to lunch. 

>2. My other problem was where I was trying to replicate the
>'scrolling' of my screens. All of my screens have nine rows that allow
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>very clunky. How should I have done this Pete?

You can pass two parameters. To get the second page, say LIMIT 9 SKIP 9.
Make sure to use an ORDER BY that has no duplicates.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2008-05-29T18:27:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com> <g7cn34hu5v1jo5rio2ve86pftn2ef7j4rp@4ax.com> <p9vn34tortqlb8ie1g789qjdcf255kvd98@4ax.com> <810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com>`

```
>>> On 5/27/2008 at 11:39 AM, in message
<g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com>, Robert<no@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 27 May 2008 06:04:08 -0700 (PDT), razor
<iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk> 
> wrote:
> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> out to lunch. 

This is what is referred to as 'optimisitic locking'.  Kind of a misnomer,
in my opinion, as it doesn't really lock it at all.

Personally, I think it's a better idea than you make it sound.  The theory
behind it is that generally there *won't* be two users at the same time
trying to update the same record.  We're being optimistic.

DB2 9.5 offers some specific support for it, so that there's no need to make
sure you remember to update a "row_version" field.

create table customers (
    SSN CHAR(9) NOT NULL
  , LAST_UPDATE TIMESTAMP NOT NULL 
                GENERATED ALWAYS FOR EACH ROW ON UPDATE
                AS ROW CHANGE TIMESTAMP
)

SELECT ssn
     , ROW CHANGE TOKEN FOR c
     , RID_BIT(c)
into :ssn
   , :rct
   , :rid-bit
FROM customers AS c;

MOVE new-ssn to ssn

UPDATE customers AS c
SET SSN = :ssn
WHERE RID_BIT(c) = :rid_bit
  AND ROW CHANGE TOKEN FOR c = :rct

The use of the RID_BIT function isn't required, but it makes finding the
record to update faster because it's a direct read; it doesn't even need to
check the index.

If someone else has updated the record since this program did it's select
then the value of the ROW CHANGE TOKEN will have changed (because of the ROW
CHANGE TIMESTAMP generated column), and therefore the update will fail (just
as with your example).  (Actually I think it says that the ROW CHANGE
TIMESTAMP column is not required, but if not present there could be false
negatives.

Here's more information that might be of interest.
http://www.ibm.com/developerworks/db2/library/techarticle/dm-0801schuetz/ind
ex.html 

I think its a pretty great thing, though I haven't done anything with it
other than a little testing.

Frank
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-05-30T01:36:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g1nlmj$4u1$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com> <483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
In article <483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

[snip]

>DB2 9.5 offers some specific support for it, so that there's no need to make
>sure you remember to update a "row_version" field.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>negatives.

Everything old is new again... but first, some musing.

A primary key is not something that gets changed lightly... how often do 
folks change SSNs?  Be that as it may... what I was taught about locking 
and checking for competition for records came from folks who coded airline 
reservations systems, aways back when.

Their underlying philosophy was 'every even-numbered agent is trying to 
get a seat immediately and every odd-numbered agent has just done a lookup 
and gone to lunch.'  An aircraft's seating status would be represented 
with a single record and in that record was the I071M28-LAST-UPDT field.

The agent would call up the aircraft's seating record and - like many a 
good field - I071M28-LAST-UPDT would be MOVEd to PREV-I071M28-LAST-UPDT.  
Then, after chatting with the customer for a bit... this one's nice, 
there's still something in smoking... the agent would prepare for update.  
Part of that preparation consisted of READing the same record again and 
checking I071M28-LAST-UPDT.  If it was the same as PREV-I071M28-LAST-UPDT 
then Robert is your mother's brother and away you go, if the 
I071M28-LAST-UPDT is greater than the PREV-I071M28-LAST-UPDT then you know 
somebody got to that flight first and the seats available are not the 
seats that you think might be available.

DD
```

```
In article <484040CA.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
>>>> On 5/29/2008 at 7:36 PM, in message <g1nlmj$4u1$1@reader2.panix.com>,
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
>> In article <483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>,
>> Frank Swarbrick <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:

[snip]

>> A primary key is not something that gets changed lightly... how often do 
>> folks change SSNs?

[snip]

>For what it's worth, I used SSN simply as an example.

Nothing is absolutely true in all cases at all times, Mr Swarbrick, 
including this statement... but, in general, a primary key is not 
something that gets changed lightly.  This should not be interpreted as 
'design your system as though primary keys never change'... but it should 
also be kept in mind that 'what should we do in order to implement a 
primary key change?' might not be a major design concern, as well.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-30T19:53:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ed6144dgrd0cjeq6ommqiipuvjdcvufrht@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com> <483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <g1nlmj$4u1$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Fri, 30 May 2008 01:36:19 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:


>A primary key is not something that gets changed lightly... how often do 
>folks change SSNs?  

How many times did Frito-Lay change the UPC of a product? Every time the price changed.
(The price was preprinted on the bag of chips, in blatant violation of the Robinson-Patman
Act.) 

Now many times to people change their Billing Telephone Number (BTN)? Frequently. Telcos
do not use the BTN+CustCode (3 digit) as primary key. They use an internally assigned
Billing Account Number (BAN), that never changes. 

How many times to people change their credit card number? Every time they lose it or
suspect fraud. 

Do you have any idea how many tables are linked to a product, phone number or credit card
account? Typically, dozens. If a natural key were used as primary, dozens of tables would
have to be updated when the primary changed. Some of those tables, such as telephone
usage, contain billions of rows. 

>Be that as it may... what I was taught about locking 
>and checking for competition for records came from folks who coded airline 
>reservations systems, aways back when.

It sounds like you're referring to the mortibund ACS/TPF operating system that required
all applications to be written in assembly language. 

>Their underlying philosophy was 'every even-numbered agent is trying to 
>get a seat immediately and every odd-numbered agent has just done a lookup 
>and gone to lunch.'  An aircraft's seating status would be represented 
>with a single record and in that record was the I071M28-LAST-UPDT field.

The field name is SO mainframesque. 

>The agent would call up the aircraft's seating record and - like many a 
>good field - I071M28-LAST-UPDT would be MOVEd to PREV-I071M28-LAST-UPDT.  
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>seats that you think might be available.

The user interface was text in teletype style and cryptic as hell. I'll bet converting it
to and from GUI on Travelocity and Expedia, both of which feed off Sabre, was a barrel of
laughs.
```

>the Robinson-Patman Act.) 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

That seems to reinforce my assertion, Mr Wagner; the Telcos realise that a 
primary key is not something that gets changed lightly so they design 
their own key which, by your own assertion, 'never changes'.

>How many times to people change their credit card number? Every time
>they lose it or suspect fraud. 

People do not change their cards, Mr Wagner, the issuing institutions 
change them.  The last time I worked in a plastic-mill they kept track of 
the changes in their Master File, a KSDS keyed on card number... and when 
the record contained only another card number another READ was issued... 
and when the next record contained only another card number yet another 
READ was issued... got moderately tedious, it did.

>
>Do you have any idea how many tables are linked to a product, phone
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>usage, contain billions of rows. 

That may be a reason for considering a primary key to be 'not something 
that gets changed lightly'.

>
>>Be that as it may... what I was taught about locking 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>all applications to be written in assembly language. 

Not only coded in ASM, Mr Wagner... but a sort of pre-RISC RISC in that 
the programmers were limited to using RR instructions.

>
>>Their underlying philosophy was 'every even-numbered agent is trying to 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The field name is SO mainframesque. 

Wow, is it?  Who might have noticed?

>
>>The agent would call up the aircraft's seating record and - like many a 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>laughs. 

Anything worthwhile takes work, Mr Wagner... that which requires little 
effort may well be worth little effort.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-31T02:41:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ds144dkt50hgc6g70sp1j9n8jgantml29@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com> <g1nlmj$4u1$1@reader2.panix.com> <ed6144dgrd0cjeq6ommqiipuvjdcvufrht@4ax.com> <g1q9rr$dpv$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Sat, 31 May 2008 01:32:43 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <ed6144dgrd0cjeq6ommqiipuvjdcvufrht@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>a product identical to others except for UPC.

Sales history, in-flight orders and all production data are moved to the new UPC.

>>Now many times to people change their Billing Telephone Number (BTN)?
>>Frequently. Telcos
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>their own key which, by your own assertion, 'never changes'.

The primary key, BAN, is not a natural key. The customer never sees it.

I forgot to mention 'NPA splits', which change the area code of hundreds of thousands of
customers simultaneously. That's an excellent example of why you shouldn't use a natural
key.

>>How many times to people change their credit card number? Every time
>>they lose it or suspect fraud. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>READ was issued... got moderately tedious, it did.

We did the same at the credit card company I managed. In retrospect, we should have
assigned a key rather than using card number. 

>>Do you have any idea how many tables are linked to a product, phone
>>number or credit card
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>that gets changed lightly'.

When you tell a corner office idiot that a key CAN be changed, with difficulty, don't be
surprised when he wants to change a hundred thousand of them. 

>>>Their underlying philosophy was 'every even-numbered agent is trying to 
>>>get a seat immediately and every odd-numbered agent has just done a lookup 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Wow, is it?  Who might have noticed?

It's ironic to hear mainfraimers bitch about foreigners taking their jobs when they,
themselves, use broken English. Their propensity for abbreviation had a simple cause: two
fingered typing. When I was a manager, I sent two fingered typists to high school adult
education classes. They were resentful at the time, but thanked me later. 

>>>The agent would call up the aircraft's seating record and - like many a 
>>>good field - I071M28-LAST-UPDT would be MOVEd to PREV-I071M28-LAST-UPDT.  
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>effort may well be worth little effort.

That's rationalization supporting bad technology. It died not because it was bad but
because it was out of date. It would still be alive if someone had ported it to z/OS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-05-31T14:08:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g1rm4r$kd3$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <ed6144dgrd0cjeq6ommqiipuvjdcvufrht@4ax.com> <g1q9rr$dpv$1@reader2.panix.com> <3ds144dkt50hgc6g70sp1j9n8jgantml29@4ax.com>`

```
In article <3ds144dkt50hgc6g70sp1j9n8jgantml29@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Sat, 31 May 2008 01:32:43 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>new UPC.

And there you have it.  A primary key is not something that gets changed 
lightly, the UPC gets changed frequently, the UPC is not something that 
should be used as a primary key.

(What you describe is confusing, though... if the UPC changes when the 
price changes and sales history records price... then how would the sales 
history showing that on 28 Feb 2004 a bag of Fritos was sold on Flight 287 
for US$.63 change when the UPC changed from 5846321975 to 7924813564 
because a month later the price went up to US$.67?  The history is the 
history.)

>
>>>Now many times to people change their Billing Telephone Number (BTN)?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>The primary key, BAN, is not a natural key. The customer never sees it.

I do not believe this situation causes any disadvantage.

>
>I forgot to mention 'NPA splits', which change the area code of hundreds
>of thousands of
>customers simultaneously. That's an excellent example of why you
>shouldn't use a natural key.

The 'nature' of different situations might not be the same, Mr Wagner... 
for instance, the nature of an individual's Social Security number is that 
it changes rarely while the nature of a person's credit-card number is 
that it can change with a shift in interest rates.

The nature of a primary key is that it should not be changed frequently... 
for reasons similar to what you've given above.

>
>>>How many times to people change their credit card number? Every time
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>assigned a key rather than using card number. 

20/20, that hindsight.  You weren't doing that in South Bend, IN, by any 
chance?

>
>>>Do you have any idea how many tables are linked to a product, phone
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>surprised when he wants to change a hundred thousand of them. 

Given that I've mentioned 'if something can be done Just This Once it can 
be done Forever' I believe I may be aware of such possibilities, Mr 
Wagner; my usual response is to stare at the ceiling, furrow my brow, work 
my lips soundlessly for a second-and-a-half... and then say 'Well, if you 
put it *that* way... it will be very, very expensive but once I see the 
task order number for the job I can start charging time towards it.'

Corner Office Idiots are notorious for ignoring what they want to ignore; 
they can turn 'it will cost money and require new bodies but we can get it 
done in two months' to 'we can get it done in two days'... so when such an 
Idiot responds to my wording above with 'Does that mean you can do it?' I 
can reply 'I said I need to see the task order, first, before charging 
time towards it... deal with that and the rest might follow.

>
>>>>Their underlying philosophy was 'every even-numbered agent is trying to 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>simple cause: two fingered typing.

Mr Wagner, it is unpleasant to hear *anyone* complain about foreigners 
taking their jobs; what you see as 'broken English' might be seen by 
others as a holdover from days when no more than eight characters could be 
used as a label.

[snip]

>>Anything worthwhile takes work, Mr Wagner... that which requires little 
>>effort may well be worth little effort.
>
>That's rationalization supporting bad technology.

Were it not more than that, Mr Wagner, all knowledge of value might be 
instantaneously gained, all activities of worth completed upon conception 
and gourmet cookery indistinguishable from microwaved muck.

As what Menaechmus is reported to have said to Alexander the Great is 
often translated, 'O king, through the country there are royal roads and 
roads for common citizens, but in geometry there is one road for all.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-31T12:48:58-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<5vu244p0dc0o4ml49ba96evmi0t6cr1k0g@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <ed6144dgrd0cjeq6ommqiipuvjdcvufrht@4ax.com> <g1q9rr$dpv$1@reader2.panix.com> <3ds144dkt50hgc6g70sp1j9n8jgantml29@4ax.com> <g1rm4r$kd3$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Sat, 31 May 2008 14:08:27 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <3ds144dkt50hgc6g70sp1j9n8jgantml29@4ax.com>,
>Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>history.)

Price changes are an issue for every product, independent of UPC changes. At the product
level, the number of primary interest is units sold rather than dollars. That switches to
money at higher levels of aggregation. Historical amounts are sometimes converted to
'constant dollars' for comparison. 

>>>>Now many times to people change their Billing Telephone Number (BTN)?
>>>>Frequently. Telcos
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>for reasons similar to what you've given above.

The shortage of telephone numbers (and IP addresses) illustrates the folly of assigning
significance to digits of an identifier. A ten digit phone number can accomodate ten
billion customers. That's greater than the number of people in the WORLD, much less the
US. We're forever running out of numbers because the first three digits mean something --
area code -- as well as the middle three digits, creatively named NXX by the telephone
industry. That may have been necessary in the Good Old Days of mechanical switching, but
telco routing has been digital, using SS7, since 1980. There is no longer a technical
requirement for geographic partitioning. 

Assignment of telephone numbers is like Cobol programming standards. Practices live on
long after their technical rationale has disappeared. Practitioners don't even remember
the reason for the standard. For instance, numbered paragraphs served as an index in the
days of hardcopy program listings. We should have stopped using them when we got text
editors in the 1970s. 

>>>>How many times to people change their credit card number? Every time
>>>>they lose it or suspect fraud. 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>chance?

Los Angeles, Carte Blanche, now a brand name of Diners Club, which is owned by Citibank.
Back in the day, we were slightly bigger than Diners.

Carte Blanche was considered the most selective and prestigious of the three T&E cards. We
rejected 50% of applications. To see how well selectivity predicted the future, we did an
experiment (at my suggestion). We randomly selected 100 applications and approved them
without checking anything except the applicant's age. Six months later we compared the
performance of those accounts to our normal ones. It was exactly the same. The company
folded because it continued rejecting 50%. Each approved application was handled by 30
people, at a cost of two times annual dues. 

>>>>Do you have any idea how many tables are linked to a product, phone
>>>>number or credit card
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>task order number for the job I can start charging time towards it.'

Doesn't your company have a Fast Track Process? That's a new name for the old way of doing
things. Agile is another name for the old way.

>Corner Office Idiots are notorious for ignoring what they want to ignore; 
>they can turn 'it will cost money and require new bodies but we can get it 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>time towards it... deal with that and the rest might follow.

What irks me is managers' refusal to believe there are logical errors in production
programs, even though errors leap out from a cursory glance at the source code. Managers
say it's impossible because the program has been tested six ways, plus has been in
production for years. Their eyes glaze when shown the errant code, as though they're being
asked to read cuneiform. 

>>>Anything worthwhile takes work, Mr Wagner... that which requires little 
>>>effort may well be worth little effort.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>roads for common citizens, but in geometry there is one road for all.'

Kings don't do geometry. There are dukes, lords and freemen between the monarch and his
geometers.
```

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-05-29T21:40:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com> <g7cn34hu5v1jo5rio2ve86pftn2ef7j4rp@4ax.com> <p9vn34tortqlb8ie1g789qjdcf255kvd98@4ax.com> <810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com> <483EF58A.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
On Thu, 29 May 2008 18:27:22 -0600, "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
wrote:

>>>> On 5/27/2008 at 11:39 AM, in message
><g8ho3494s3qhig424o3iivveqm6fp4onjb@4ax.com>, Robert<no@e.mail> wrote:
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>trying to update the same record.  We're being optimistic.

That's valid in many/most cases. Two customer service reps cannot be talking to the
customer at the same time. Mary handles vendors whose names begin with A through M, Susie
handles N through Z. 

It's not valid in batch processing using multiple processes nor on communal resources such
as inventory. But collisions can often be handled more gracefully than simply failing the
update. Suppose 2 order takers are processing orders for widgets. A pulls it up and sees
quantity available 1,000. B enters an order for 10. A enters an order for 1. A's update
fails because the quantity changed, it is no longer 1,000. Big deal, 990 is still adequate
to process an order for 1. The application should re-fetch quantity available, see that it
is adequate, and update it to 989. It should NOT make the order taker re-enter the order.

>DB2 9.5 offers some specific support for it, so that there's no need to make
>sure you remember to update a "row_version" field.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>)

Neat. In most databases we have to write a trigger.

>SELECT ssn
>     , ROW CHANGE TOKEN FOR c
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>check the index.

In my example, I accidentally forgot to show ROWID as the row finder. I intended to say:

   where rowid = :hv-rowid
      and  row_version = :hv-row-varsion

There is a gotcha with that technique. In Oracle, if the table has a primary key and
someone changes the key, the ROWID will change. As a result, the update will fail. 
If there is no primary key, ROWID will never change. 

That's not a serious concern for updates, but it means tables cannot be joined using
ROWID. 

>If someone else has updated the record since this program did it's select
>then the value of the ROW CHANGE TOKEN will have changed (because of the ROW
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>negatives.

Some will argue that a TIMESTAMP doesn't have enough resolution. It's a function of the
operating system's clock resolution and the database's TIMESTAMP format. It has been 1/100
second on every system I worked on. That's good enough for human operators but not good
enough for batch, where we can process 100 transactions in 1/100 second. 

>Here's more information that might be of interest.
>http://www.ibm.com/developerworks/db2/library/techarticle/dm-0801schuetz/ind
>ex.html 

That page shows DB2 timer resolution of 1/1,000,000 second. That's good enough.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions on approach, please...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-05-28T12:33:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a3nimF35sqduU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<69s1u6F3438eiU1@mid.individual.net> <8d9m34h0rhsnnb00r0c9t6mkv7eh3pbltk@4ax.com> <t5vm34t5gtsfc2mv4qce963d8nra23bcu3@4ax.com> <g7cn34hu5v1jo5rio2ve86pftn2ef7j4rp@4ax.com> <p9vn34tortqlb8ie1g789qjdcf255kvd98@4ax.com> <810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```


"razor" <iruddock@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:810c2e53-88be-4cac-b545-84ebd69574ca@34g2000hsh.googlegroups.com...
> Hi Pete
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> SQL knowledge is limited and I may be missing something here.

Would you hold a record lock in an application for 10 minutes? I don't think 
so, even if you were working in an environment where you could.
(Mainframe environments using TP monitors like CICS and what used to be 
IMS/DC, dequeue as soon as they respond to the terminal, and at this point 
all locks are lost... A PC COULD hold a lock indefinitely, but it would be 
poor application design to do so. Some applications address this by 
re-fetching the record immediately before update.)

To answer your question: In a RDBMS system the RDBMS takes care of it. (You 
can "help it out" by specifying things like "WITH UPDATE INTENT". )

>
> 2. My other problem was where I was trying to replicate the
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> very clunky.

You may be being a little hard on yourself :-)

COBOL uses a hierarchic data system, the relational model doesn't. This 
causes problems when converting. If you were building a RDB from scratch you 
probably wouldn't use split keys, but you're not, so I don't think your 
solution is so terrible.

I build RDBs from COBOL data definitions. That means they have group fields 
on the database. There are two choices:

1. Remove the group field and amend the code that accesses it.
2. Retain the group field and make sure that it is updated when one of its 
components changes (maybe a stored procedure...)

(My data conversion software can flag the names of group fields as an 
option. This means they can be readily identified on the RDB, but it also 
means that all existing code which uses them will fail. Individual 
circumstances will dictate what you do about it and it usually comes down to 
choosing the lesser of two evils. Life is about choices... :-))

>How should I have done this Pete?
>
I'm not well enough qualified in this area to tell you how you SHOULD have 
done it. (Remember there is seldom only ONE "right" way. For me, if 
something works then that's pretty good. If it doesn't work well, then look 
at improving it :-))

I CAN tell you that, given what you have described, I would probably have 
done what you did :-).

Pete.
```
